title: Christmas storms across Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria
description: AFCA has activated its significant event response plan following the Christmas storms across Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria being declared a significant event on 27 December 2023 by the Insurance Council of Australia (ICA).
body: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) has activated its significant event response plan following the Christmas storms across Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria being declared a significant event on 27 December 2023 by the Insurance Council of Australia (ICA). Our significant event response plan is activated for events that can potentially result in significant numbers of related complaints coming to AFCA. It provides for early communication with relevant stakeholders and a more streamlined, expedited process for the resolution of related complaints. Have you been affected? If you have been affected by the Christmas storms across Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria, we encourage you to contact your insurance company. For help with the claims process, or if you are unable to contact your insurance company, please call the Insurance Council of Australia disaster hotline on 1800 734 621. How we can help you AFCA offers free and accessible dispute resolution services to consumers and small businesses impacted by this event. If you have raised a complaint with your insurance company but you have been unable to resolve the matter, you can then come to us for assistance. Please note, we are only able to consider your complaint once you have raised the matter with your insurer. If you encounter difficulties relating to your insurance claims which you are unable to resolve directly with your insurer, you can register your complaint with us using our online complaint form or by calling 1800 931 678. More information about the process we follow to resolve complaints is available on our website. Financial hardship People affected by natural disasters can experience both short-term and long-term financial difficulties, ranging from lost credit cards and problems accessing cash through to being unable to make loan repayments. Banks and other financial services providers generally provide assistance to customers in cases of genuine hardship. If you encounter difficulties relating to a financial hardship application, you can make a complaint to us online, or call us on 1800 931 678. We have also shared a factsheet on natural disasters and financial hardship. Please make sure you notify us that you have been affected by the Christmas storms when you make your complaint. Support for First Nations communities AFCA is committed to providing a service that is accessible to everyone. We have strong relationships with organisations that support First Nations communities across Australia, to ensure First Nations communities are aware of their right to complain to the financial ombudsman. You can find out more about our accessibility and additional support services here. Other help Mob Strong, Debt Help - Part of the Financial Rights Legal Centre, Mob Strong, Debt Help is a free legal advice service about money matters for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples from anywhere in Australia. ICAN - The Indigenous Consumer Assistance Network

title: Claims Leaders Summit 2026
body: Welcome and introduction Good morning everyone, and welcome to this year’s Claims Leader’s Summit. My name is Deborah Jenkins, I’m the inaugural Chief Customer Officer at the Australian Financial Complaints Authority, and I’m really pleased to be here with you this morning to open the summit. As Chief Customer Officer, I’m responsible for all of our frontline teams – our customer service area, our case management and the customer experience for all customers – members and consumers. Before I begin, I’d like to acknowledge the Traditional Custodians of the land we’re meeting on today, the Gadigal people of the Eora Nation, and pay my respects to their Elders past and present. I extend that respect to any Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people joining us today. I’ve just spent last week up in Gimuy and the lands of the Gimuy Walubara Yidinji peoples at the Financial Counsellors Australia conference and a really important aspect of my time there was hearing firsthand First Nations peoples experience of the financial system – a timely reminder to me of the work that we have all got to do to ensure that our services are designed to be inclusive and take into account lived experiences of those using them. This really links to the theme of my speech today. I’ve been in this role at AFCA for coming up to 3 months now, and I wanted to begin today by sharing some reflections from that time. Not prescriptions. Not instructions. And not criticisms. But reflections: as someone who has stepped into this role at a time of real pressure across the financial system, and who has had the privilege of listening closely to consumers, members like you, and our own people at AFCA. I don’t wish to be Captain Obvious here, but what’s struck me most since I joined is this: it is an exceptionally challenging time for both industry and Australian consumers. And it is an exceptionally demanding time to be working in dispute resolution. The pressure is real. It has been building for some time. And it is being felt right across the system. Consumers are under strain, often long before they ever pick up the phone to lodge a complaint. Claims teams are operating in an environment of growing complexity, scrutiny and expectation. And organisations like AFCA, sitting between consumers and industry, are managing higher volumes, more complexity, and greater vulnerability than we’ve seen before. It’s no coincidence that tonight’s Federal Budget will be grappling with many of these same pressures at a national level: how we build resilience in an uncertain world, how we manage rising cost‑of‑living pressures, how we lift productivity and reform our tax system, and how we support people through periods of economic stress. Those same themes play out every day in the claims system: in how quickly issues are resolved, how clearly expectations are set, and how effectively pressure is released before matters escalate. So today, I don’t want to talk about what insurers should be doing.

title: Class actions affecting AFCA complaints
description: Discover how class actions impact complaints made to AFCA and what steps to take if your complaint is affected. Click here to read more.
body: Jump to: What is a class action? Financial class actions Class action ‘opt out’ How AFCA deals with complaints relating to class actions Our role We may exclude a complaint Relevant information From financial firms From complainants If you have opted out If you are not sure If you think you fall outside the definition of the class Click the button below to download a PDF copy of the factsheet. Factsheet - Class actions affecting AFCA complaints This factsheet outlines how AFCA deals with complaints relating to class actions, including when we might exclude a complaint that relates to a class action. It also covers class actions more broadly and outlines the type of information we will ask for. What is a class action? In Australia, class actions are court proceedings where a representative (the applicant), on behalf of a group of people (class members), make claims against another person or organisation Financial class actions In recent years, there has been a growing number of class actions filed against financial firms. We are likely to see this trend continue given the issues raised in the Royal Commission into Misconduct in the Banking, Superannuation and Financial Services Industry. Class action ‘opt out’ Class actions in both the Federal Court of Australia and state/territory supreme courts use an ‘opt out’ model. This means: In class actions, the court will ask the applicant to send an opt out notice to all known class members who have the same interest in the class action. If any class member does not want to be included in the class action, they must return the opt out notice to the court by the set date. If a class member does not return the opt out notice by the set date, they continue to be a member of the class action. This is the case even if they had not received a copy of the opt out notice and do not know that there is a class action taking place. A sample Federal Court opt out notice can be found here: fedcourt.gov.au/law-andpractice/practice-documents/practicenotes/gpn-ca/sample-opt-out-notice Anyone who falls within the definition of the class becomes a class member, regardless of whether they know about it or not. All class members are bound by any court judgment or court-approved settlement, unless they opt out of the class action before a certain date as set by the court (the set date). How AFCA deals with complaints relating to class actions Our role External dispute resolution is an alternative to tribunals and courts to resolve complaints individuals and small businesses have with their financial firms. Where an AFCA complainant is already a part of a class action, it is not appropriate for both the court and AFCA to deal with the same complaint. We may exclude a complaint We have discretion under our Rules to exclude some complaints. We do not exercise our discretion lightly and will only do so where there are compelling reasons. In some cases, we may not be aware of the class action. We rely on financial firms to tell u

title: Clearance ratio
body: The clearance ratio measures the number of complaints that were closed out of all the complaints received. What does it show? A clearance ratio of less than 100% indicates that AFCA has received more complaints against a member than it has resolved in the same period. This means the number of complaints open at AFCA about this financial firm has increased during the period. Over time, a constant clearance ratio of less than 100% will result in a financial firm having an increasing number of open complaints with AFCA. This may place strain on the financial firm’s effectiveness to resolve complaints directly with their customers because the financial firm’s open caseload of complaints is growing. A clearance ratio of over 100% indicates that more open complaints were closed during the period than new complaints were received. This can be an indication that the financial firm's internal dispute resolution function is well-resourced and effective at reducing the need for their customers to escalate their complaint to AFCA. It may also reflect a change to complaint patterns due to the introduction or removal of financial services practices or products. How the clearance ratio is calculated The clearance ratio is calculated using the following formula: Clearance ratio (%) = complaints closed / complaints received X 100 Best practice Effective IDR to resolve complaints directly with the complainant Ensure your dispute resolution teams have the right mix of human skills, subject matter expertise, time management skills and communication skills Ensure your customers know who within your organisation is responsible for their complaint. Early engagement and regular updates and interaction will give your customers confidence that their concern is being treated seriously, minimising the likelihood of the matter being escalated to AFCA whilst it is under review. If the review of their complaint is taking longer than initially anticipated, updating your customer will assist in managing their expectations. Have a genuine willingness to approach a complaint with a fresh set of eyes and an appreciation that a complaint resolved early often enhances a customer’s future engagement with the firm. Engaging with AFCA's process and resolving complaints efficiently Treat the refer-back stage as a new opportunity to talk to the complainant and look at the complaint from a fresh perspective Contact the consumer by telephone early in the referral timeframe. This is an opportunity to discuss the whole complaint and find out details that could be missed in written correspondence If a resolution is reached, ensure the resolution is clear for all parties and provide a copy to AFCA If you are unable to reach a resolution, consider the information which will assist AFCA to consider the complaint and provide this prior to the conclusion of the refer-back period. title: Code compliance and monitoring
description: Code compliance and monitoring
body: The Code group provides operational and program delivery services to five independent Code Compliance Committees. It plays an important role in driving improved industry practice, improving customer outcomes and reducing the likelihood of disputes. The role of the Code group Under the strategic direction of five Independent Code Compliance Committees, the Code group is responsible for monitoring and driving best practice compliance with industry Codes of Practice. The Code group: The Code group does not resolve disputes or provide individual customer outcomes. Examines the practices of the financial firms that subscribe to a Code (subscribers) Identifies and report on current and emerging industry-wide problems Reviews reported breaches Recommends improved practices – focusing on system, process, and governance improvements Refers sanction considerations to Committees for serious compliance failures Consults and keeps stakeholders and the public informed. The relationship between the Code group and AFCA The Code group is an independent business unit with its own funding that benefits from sharing certain services and infrastructure with AFCA. The Code group meets regularly with a range of stakeholders, including AFCA teams to discuss industry issues, trends and provide updates on Code group workplans and activities. AFCA has an interest in ensuring Codes of Practice are strong, robust, properly supported and developed. Compliance with strong Codes of Practice help bring about fair outcomes and reduce the likelihood of disputes and complaints. Codes of Practice Codes of Practice are contemporary conduct models established in consultation with a variety of stakeholders and owned by the industries. They are a form of self-regulation that provide important safeguards and protections that often extend beyond the law. The Codes of Practice contain obligations that set certain standards of service and behaviour from Code-subscribing institutions. Subscribing to the Code commits an institution to good practices, informed decision-making, and service delivery that is fair and reasonable. The Codes are an important part of the national consumer protection framework and financial services regulatory system. Learn more about each Code of Practice and the Compliance Committee that oversees it: Banking Code Compliance Committee Customer Owned Banking Code Compliance Committee General Insurance Code Governance Committee Insurance Brokers Code Compliance Committee Life Code Compliance Committee For all media enquiries, please email media@codecompliance.org.au

title: Code Compliance publications
description: Code Compliance publications
body: The Code Team supports independent committees to monitor compliance with codes of practice in the Australian financial services industry, and to achieve service standards that people can trust. The committees’ publications, including annual compliance reviews, inquiry reports and guidance notes, offer in-depth analysis as well as valuable guidance on code compliance within the banking, customer owned banking, general insurance, insurance broking and life insurance industries. title: Come presentare un reclamo finanziario con AFCA
body: Tutto su AFCA L’Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) è un programma di risoluzione delle dispute esterno e indipendente. AFCA offre agli individui e alle piccole imprese un servizio gratuito, equo e flessibile per la risoluzione dei reclami sui prodotti e servizi finanziari. Possiamo gestire una vasta gamma di problemi, inclusi: errori nelle transizioni bancarie e negli elenchi creditizi difficoltà a ripagare i prestiti, le carte di credito e la finanza a breve termine ove la vostra posizione finanziaria sia cambiata richiesta di risarcimento negata da parte dell’assicurazione (ad es. automobile, casa e contenuti, animali domestici, viaggio, tutela del reddito e trauma) consulenza di investimento inappropriata la decisione di un amministratore fiduciario sulla distribuzione di un superannuation benefit. Che cosa possiamo fare Se non siete in grado di risolvere il reclamo direttamente con l’azienda finanziaria, potete contattare AFCA. Possiamo aiutarvi a risolvere il reclamo senza i costi e lo stress di rivolgervi a un tribunale. Non è necessario ottenere consulenza legale o di altro tipo, a meno che non lo desideriate. Cosa posso fare se sto affrontando delle difficoltà finanziarie? Gli individui e le piccole imprese a volte possono trovarsi in situazioni in cui non sono in grado di rispettare le condizioni di rimborso di una linea di credito (come un mutuo per la casa, un prestito aziendale o una carta di credito). Ciò viene definito "difficoltà finanziaria". Guida per gestire le difficoltà finanziarie Servizio di interpretariato Se l’inglese non è la vostra prima lingua, possiamo organizzare l’accesso al servizio gratuito di interpretariato. Chiamate il servizio gratuito di interpretariato al 131 450, o chiamateci al 1800 931 678* e organizzeremo un interprete per voi. Contattateci Per parlare con il nostro personale chiamate 1800 931 678*. *9am–5pm ora locale di Melbourne. Le chiamate a questo numero dai telefoni fissi sono gratuite. Potrebbero applicarsi tariffe per le chiamate dai cellulari, si prega di verificare con il proprio operatore di telefonia mobile. Per maggiori informazioni Come risolvere un reclamo Modulo di reclamo – versione stampabile Guida alle conferenze di conciliazione Guida per gestire le difficoltà finanziarie Come risolvere un reclamo relativo a piccole imprese Feedback sul nostro servizio La vostra opinione è importante. I vostri complimenti, suggerimenti e reclami sul nostro servizio ci aiutano a migliorare. Ci impegniamo a fornire a tutti i nostri clienti un servizio eccellente, ma sappiamo che a volte le cose possono andare per il verso sbagliato. Se questo dovesse succedere, potete farcelo sapere. Feedback e reclami sul servizio dell'AFCA Altri contatti Australian Financial Complaints Authority GPO Box 3 Melbourne VIC 3001 Email: info@afca.org.au Fax: (03) 9613 6399 Sito web: www.afca.org.au Ascolta il file audio: Come AFCA può essere d’aiuto Un file audio che spiega come AFCA può aiutare le persone a r

title: Comentarios y quejas sobre el servicio de la AFCA
body: Valoramos sus comentarios (retroalimentación). Sus cumplidos, sugerencias y quejas en relación a nuestro servicio nos ayudan a mejorar. Nos empeñamos en brindar un excelente servicio a todos nuestros clientes, pero sabemos que a veces las cosas salen mal. Si esto sucede, hagánoslo saber. Esta página le explica cómo: Nuestro procedimiento para manejar quejas y comentarios está más enfocado en cómo brindamos nuestro servicio y no cubre situaciones en las que usted se muestre insatisfecho únicamente por la resolución de una queja presentada ante una empresa financiera, (incluyendo cualquier determinación final relacionada con alguna queja). Cuando la AFCA emite una determinación, ésta es una decisión final en relación a la queja. No pueda apelarse por medio de la AFCA. hacer sus comentarios en relación al manejo de alguna queja, y/o presentar una queja sobre nuestro servicio al cliente. Cómo puede darnos sus comentarios Si no necesita una respuesta directa de parte nuestra, la forma más sencilla de compartir sus comentarios es completando nuestro formulario en línea y seleccionando “cumplido” o “sugerencia”. También puede compartir sus comentarios sobre su experiencia con nosotros por correo electrónico, por teléfono o por escrito. Cómo puede quejarse sobre nuestro servicio Si usted se queja con nosotros sobre algún servicio que le hayamos brindado, lo investigaremos y trataremos de resolver el problema directamente con usted. Pero si no podemos, el asesor independiente de la AFCA podría evaluar su queja de servicio de forma independiente y hacer alguna recomendación para abordar cualquier problema con el mismo. Para quejarse sobre nuestro servicio, usted puede: Si usted no está seguro sobre con quién contactar, llámenos al 1800 931 678 y le pondremos en contacto con alguien que pueda ayudarle. Primero que todo, hablar con la persona con quien ha estado tratando. Por lo general ésta será la persona que manejará su caso ya sea usted consumidor o miembro de alguna empresa financiera. La mayor parte del tiempo, su asunto se podrá resolver de forma inmediata. Si usted siente que no hemos resuelto su problema, también puede pedir a algún gerente que revise su queja sobre el servicio en cuestión. Por otro lado, puede completar el formulario de retroalimentación en línea y seleccionar la opción “quejas relacionadas con el servicio de la AFCA”. Unos días laborales después recibirá un número de referencia separado. Cómo manejamos quejas de nuestros servicios Si usted presenta una queja en relación a alguno de nuestros servicios, investigaremos y tomaremos sus inquietudes en consideración y responderemos a las mismas. Si su queja requiere de mayor acción, trabajaremos con usted para encontrar una solución adecuada. Las quejas relacionadas con nuestro servicio deberán ser presentadas en un plazo de seis meses después de cerrado el caso relacionado con la queja ante la compañía financiera. Servicios sobre los cuales usted puede quejarse Podemos considerar queja

title: Comment déposer une réclamation auprès de l'AFCA
body: Qu'est-ce que l'AFCA ? L'Autorité australienne des réclamations dans le domaine de la finance (AFCA) est un mécanisme indépendant externe de résolution des litiges. L'AFCA fournit aux particuliers et aux petites entreprises un service gratuit, équitable et indépendant pour résoudre les litiges concernant les produits et services financiers. Nous pouvons traiter une grande variété de problèmes, en particulier : les erreurs commises dans les opérations bancaires et les dossiers de crédit les difficultés à rembourser les prêts, les financements à court terme ou sur carte de crédit lorsque votre situation financière a changé les demandes de règlement refusées par les assurances (automobile, habitation, animaux de compagnie, voyage, protection du revenu et traumatismes) conseils inappropriés en matière d'investissement décision d'un syndic concernant la distribution d'une prestation de retraite Ce que nous pouvons faire S'il vous est impossible de résoudre votre litige directement avec votre compagnie financière, vous pouvez contacter l'AFCA. Nous pouvons vous aider à résoudre votre litige sans les coûts et le stress des procédures judiciaires. Il n'est pas nécessaire d'obtenir un avis juridique ou autre pour enregistrer votre réclamation, à moins que vous ne le souhaitiez. Que faire si je rencontre des difficultés financières? Les particuliers et les petites entreprises peuvent parfois se trouver dans des situations où ils sont incapables d'honorer leurs obligations de remboursement des crédits qui leur ont été consentis (tels que prêt au logement, prêt commercial ou carte de crédit). C'est ce que nous appelons difficulté financière. Guide pour gérer les situations de difficulté financière Service d'interprétariat Si l'anglais n'est pas votre première langue, nous pouvons organiser un service gratuit d'interprétariat. Pour appeler le service gratuit d'interprétariat, composez le 131 450 ou appelez-nous au 1800 931 678* et nous vous fournirons un interprète. Pour nous contacter Pour parler à un membre de notre personnel appelez le 1800 931 678*. De *9 h à 17 h, heure de Melbourne. Les appels vers ce numéro depuis des postes fixes en Australie sont gratuits. Les appels à partir de portables peuvent être payants. Renseignez-vous auprès de votre opérateur. Pour plus d'information Comment résoudre un litige Formulaire de réclamation - version imprimable Guide pour les réunions de médiation Guide pour gérer les situations de difficulté financière Retour d'information sur nos services Nous apprécions vos commentaires. Les compliments, les suggestions et les plaintes concernant notre service nous aident à nous améliorer. Nous nous efforçons de fournir un excellent service à tous nos clients, mais nous sommes conscients que les choses peuvent parfois mal se passer. Si c'est le cas, vous pouvez nous en informer. Commentaires et plaintes concernant le service fourni par l'AFCA Autres coordonnées Australian Financial Complaints Authority GPO Box 3 Melbourne VIC

title: Commentaires et plaintes concernant le service fourni par l'AFCA
body: Nous apprécions vos commentaires. Les compliments, les suggestions et les plaintes concernant notre service nous aident à nous améliorer. Nous nous efforçons de fournir un excellent service à tous nos clients, mais nous sommes conscients que les choses peuvent parfois mal se passer. Si c'est le cas, vous pouvez nous en informer. Cette page explique comment : Notre procédure de plainte et de retour d'information ne concerne pas les situations d'insatisfaction de votre part à propos de l'issue d'une plainte contre une société financière (y compris une décision finale de notre part concernant une telle plainte), mais plutôt la manière dont nos services vous ont été dispensés. Toute décision rendue par l'AFCA au sujet d'une plainte est finale. Cette décision ne peut faire l'objet d'aucun appel auprès de l'AFCA. nous fournir un retour d'information sur la manière dont nous avons traité une plainte, et/ou déposer une plainte concernant notre service clientèle. Comment nous faire part de vos commentaires Si vous ne nécessitez pas de réponse de notre part, le moyen le plus simple de nous faire parvenir vos commentaires est de remplir notre formulaire en ligne en sélectionnant « compliment » ou « suggestion ». Vous pouvez également nous faire part de votre expérience par courriel, par téléphone ou par écrit. Comment déposer une plainte concernant notre service Si vous déposez une plainte concernant le service que nous vous avons fourni, nous mènerons une enquête et tenterons de résoudre le problème directement avec vous. Mais si nous n'y parvenons pas, l'évaluateur indépendant de l'AFCA peut examiner de manière indépendante votre plainte concernant le service fourni et recommander les démarches que nous devrons entreprendre pour résoudre les problèmes soulevés. Pour déposer une plainte concernant le service que vous nous avons fourni, vous pouvez : Si vous ne savez pas à qui vous adresser, appelez-nous au 1800 931 678 et nous vous mettrons en contact avec quelqu'un en mesure de vous aider. En premier lieu, informer du probléme la personne à qui vous avez eu affaire. En général, que vous soyez consommateur ou société financière membre de l'AFCA, ce sera cette personne qui se trouvera chargée de votre dossier. La plupart du temps, cette personne sera en mesure de régler immédiatement les problèmes avec vous. Si vous estimez que nous n'avons pas résolu votre problème, vous pouvez aussi demander à un supérieur hiérarchique d'examiner votre plainte. Sinon, vous pouvez remplir notre formulaire de retour d'information en ligne et sélectionner « Service complaint about AFCA ». Vous recevrez un numéro de référence individuel dans les jours ouvrables qui suivent. Comment nous traitons une plainte concernant notre service Si vous déposez une plainte concernant le service que nous avons fourni, nous procéderons à une enquête et examinerons les problèmes que vous avez soulevés, et nous y répondrons. Si votre plainte nécessite la prise de mesures supplémentaires, nous

title: Commonwealth Bank of Australia system issues
description: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) is aware that the Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA) experienced system issues in October 2024.
body: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) is aware that the Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA) experienced system issues in October 2024. The purpose of this information is to provide early communication for consumers who may have been impacted. What has happened? CBA experienced system issues in October 2024 which meant that customer transactions were duplicated. Have you been affected? AFCA encourages consumers impacted by these outages to contact CBA and discuss options to resolve any outstanding concerns. You can contact them by calling 132 221 or lodging your feedback or complaint here. We understand that system issues are frustrating and can cause inconvenience, however, AFCA can only consider complaints about system issues if a consumer has experienced loss and the complaint otherwise falls within our Rules. Some financial firms may decide to resolve a complaint by making an offer where no loss has occurred, however, this is a matter for them. If you encounter difficulties which you are unable to resolve directly with CBA, AFCA may be able to consider your complaint. You can register your complaint with AFCA by using our online complaint form or by calling 1800 931 678. The process we will follow When you complain to us, we follow a complaint resolution process that provides fair outcomes. If you lodge a complaint, AFCA will first refer the matter back to CBA so that it has an opportunity to resolve the complaint directly with you. If the complaint remains unresolved and progresses to case management, AFCA will look into whether the system error led you to suffer any loss. We will ask you to provide information and documentation as evidence of the loss. Every complaint is unique in nature and will be decided based on what happened. You can find out more about the process we follow at afca.org.au/process. AFCA’s role AFCA is a non-government ombudsman service providing free, fair and independent help with financial disputes. We will generally try to first resolve a complaint by informal methods to reach a settlement between you and the financial firm through negotiation or conciliation. If this does not work, we may then use more formal methods.

title: Commonwealth Bank of Australia system outages
description: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) is aware that the Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA) experienced system outages in June and July 2021. The purpose of this information is to provide early communication for consumers who may have been impacted.

title: Cómo hacer una denuncia financiera ante la AFCA
description: Languages-Spanish
body: Sobre la AFCA La Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) es un programa independiente de resolución externa de conflictos La AFCA proporciona a los particulares y negocios pequeños un servicio gratuito, justo y flexible para resolver las denuncias sobre productos o servicios financieros. Tratamos con una amplia gama de problemas, incluyendo: errores en las transacciones bancarias y listas de créditos dificultades para pagar préstamos, tarjetas de créditos y financiación a corto plazo en los casos que su situación financiera haya cambiado negación a un reclamo de seguro (como automotriz, de hogar y contenidos, de mascotas, de viaje, protección de los ingresos y trauma) asesoramiento inapropiado en temas de inversión una decisión del fideicomisario sobre la distribución de un beneficio de jubilación. Qué podemos hacer Si no puede resolver su denuncia directamente con su firma financiera, usted puede contactar a la AFCA. Podemos ayudarlo a resolver sus denuncias sin el costo y estrés de acudir a los tribunales. No hay necesidad de buscar asesoramiento legal o de otro tipo al presentar una denuncia, a menos que usted desee hacerlo. ¿Qué puedo hacer si tengo dificultades financieras? Las personas y pequeñas empresas algunas veces se encuentran en situaciones en las que no pueden cumplir con los requisitos de pago de una línea de crédito (por ejemplo, préstamo hipotecario, préstamo comercial o tarjeta de crédito). A esto le llamamos dificultad financiera. Guía para tratar con las dificultades financieras Servicio de interpretación Si el inglés no es su lengua materna, podemos organizar el servicio de interpretación gratuito. Llame al servicio de interpretación gratuito al 131 450, o llámenos al 1800 931 678* y organizaremos el servicio de interpretación para usted. Contáctenos Hable con nuestro personal en el 1800 931 678*. *De 9am–5pm en el horario de Melbourne. Las llamadas a este número desde líneas telefónicas fijas dentro de Australia son gratis. Las llamadas desde teléfonos móviles pueden tener un cargo, por favor consulte con su proveedor. Más información Cómo resolver un folleto de denuncias Formulario de denuncia – versión para imprimir Guía para las reuniones de conciliación Guía para tratar con las dificultades financieras Cómo resolver una denuncia de una pequeña empresa Comentarios sobre nuestro servicio Valoramos sus comentarios (retroalimentación). Sus cumplidos, sugerencias y quejas en relación a nuestro servicio nos ayudan a mejorar. Nos empeñamos en brindar un excelente servicio a todos nuestros clientes, pero sabemos que a veces las cosas salen mal. Si esto sucede, hagánoslo saber. Comentarios y quejas sobre el servicio de la AFCA Otros datos de contacto Australian Financial Complaints Authority GPO Box 3 Melbourne VIC 3001 Correo electrónico: info@afca.org.au Fax: (03) 9613 6399 Sitio web: www.afca.org.au Escuchar audio: Cómo la AFCA puede ayudar Un archivo de audio que explica cómo la AFCA asiste a las personas a resolver

title: Como posso submeter uma reclamação com a AFCA?
body: Sobre a AFCA A Autoridade Australiana para Reclamações no Sector Financeiro (AFCA) é um esquema de resolução de disputas independente. A AFCA providencia um serviço justo e independente na resolução de reclamações acerca de produtos e serviços financeiros a pessoas e pequenas empresas. Podemos lidar com uma vasta gama de problemas, incluindo: erros em transacções bancárias e listas de crédito dificuldade em pagar empréstimos, cartões de crédito e financiamentos a curto prazo em que a sua posição financeira mudou recusa num pedido de reembolso de seguro (tais como seguro automóvel, casa e recheio, animais de estimação, viagens, protecção de rendimentos e trauma) aconselhamento financeiro inadequado decisão de um administrador acerca da distribuição de um benefício relacionado com a "superannuation" (PPR) O que podemos fazer Se não conseguir resolver a sua reclamação directamente com a sua instituição financeira, pode contactar a AFCA. Podemos ajudar a resolver a sua reclamação sem os custos e o stress de ir a tribunal. Não necessita de obter apoio jurídico ou outro, quando submeter uma reclamação connosco, a menos que assim o deseje. O que posso fazer se estiver com dificuldades financeiras? Pessoas e pequenas empresas podem, por vezes, encontrar-se em situações onde são incapazes de efectuar os seus pagamentos regulares sob uma facilidade de crédito (tal como um empréstimo para habitação, empréstimo comercial ou cartão de crédito). Nós chamamos isso de dificuldades financeiras. Guia para lidar com dificuldades financeiras Serviço de intérprete Se o inglês não é a sua língua materna, podemos também arranjar o acesso a um serviço de intérprete gratuito. Ligue para a linha de serviço de intérprete gratuito através do número 131 450 ou ligue-nos através do número 1800 931 678* e nós organizaremos um interprete para si. Contacte-nos Para falar com a nossa equipa, ligue para 1800 931 678*. *das 9h às 17h, hora de Melbourne. As chamadas para este número são gratuitas, para números fixos na Austrália Chamadas a partir de telemóvel podem ser cobradas, por favor verifique com a sua operadora. Para mais informações Como resolver uma reclamação Formulário de reclamação - versão imprimível Guia para conferências de conciliação Guia para lidar com dificuldades financeiras Como resolver uma reclamação de uma pequena empresa Feedback ao nosso serviço Valorizamos o seu feedback. Elogios, sugestões e reclamações sobre o nosso serviço, ajudam-nos a melhorar. Esforçamo-nos por prestar um serviço excelente a todos os nossos clientes, mas estamos cientes que por vezes nem tudo corre bem. Se tal acontecer, pode-nos avisar. Feedback e reclamações sobre o serviço da AFCA Outros detalhes de contacto Australian Financial Complaints Authority GPO Box 3 Melbourne VIC 3001 Email: info@afca.org.au Fax: (03) 9613 6399 Página: www.afca.org.au Ouvir o audio: Como a AFCA pode ajudar Um ficheiro auditivo explicando como a AFCA ajuda as pessoas a resolver reclamações acerca de um pr

title: Compensation Scheme of Last Resort
body: On 22 June 2023, the Australian Parliament passed legislation establishing a Compensation Scheme of Last Resort (CSLR). The scheme commenced on 2 April 2024. More information including how to lodge a claim can be found on the CSLR website. The CSLR can pay up to $150,000 in compensation to eligible consumers who have received an AFCA determination awarding compensation that remains unpaid in relation to complaints in one of four areas: personal financial advice, credit intermediation, securities dealing or credit provision. The AFCA complaint process must first be completed before a claim can be lodged with the CSLR. An example overview of the process is provided below: Scope of the CSLR The CSLR provides compensation of up to $150,000 to eligible consumers who have an unpaid determination from AFCA relating to: Other financial services will be outside the scope of the CSLR, such as: Please note this is not an exhaustive list. You can view the details of the legislation here. Personal financial advice provided to retail clients on relevant financial products Dealing in securities for retail clients (but not issuing securities) Providing credit (where a financial firm provides funds) Arranging credit (where someone like a mortgage or finance broker arranges funds). Managed investment schemes (a form of collective investment where investors pool money to be managed by a third party, such as a property fund or agricultural scheme). Dealing in foreign exchange or derivatives Arranging insurance (e.g. through a broker) Funeral insurance Paused complaints update – May 2024 Over the last quarter, significant progress has been made in the processing of complaints that were paused pending the establishment of the CSLR. AFCA has now investigated and finalised almost all cases that were initially assessed as likely out of scope for CSLR or outside of AFCA Rules. We acknowledge it may be very disappointing for these people after waiting so long for an outcome. We have also continued to progress Dixon Advisory complaints. While work is well under way, it will take time to get through the large number of Dixon complaints, and we thank people for their patience. What we’ve done: Closed 97% of complaints which were outside of scope of the CSLR (1,709 complaints). Each complaint was carefully reviewed before this assessment was communicated to the consumer. Continued investigations on in-scope complaints and issued determinations for CSLR eligible complaints. Published the first lead determination for Dixon Advisory & Superannuation Services, which will guide decision making and assist efficient processing of the remaining cases. Further lead determinations on Dixon Advisory are also published here. Doubled the size of our Investments and Advice team and appointed a Senior CSLR Ombudsman to accelerate Dixon case investigations. Future updates: AFCA have now finalised all paused complaints with the exception of Dixon Advisory and Youpla. Future updates on these en

title: Compensation Scheme of Last Resort – AFCA’s Role
body: Annual Review 2024-25 Download the PDF Previous Annual Reviews Contents AFCA and the Compensation Scheme of Last Resort (CSLR) are independent bodies with distinct roles. AFCA resolves complaints and determines compensation. The CSLR is a separate, independent entity that pays eligible compensation in accordance with legislation when an AFCA determination is unpaid. While the CSLR cannot address every past instance of misconduct, going forward, it provides a crucial safety net for eligible consumers. The need for this scheme is clear. For many Australians, the consequences of poor financial advice or failed investments have been devastating, wiping out retirement savings, destabilising families, and leaving lasting emotional and financial scars. The CSLR is designed to improve trust in financial services by ensuring that, when financial firms fail and compensation is awarded but not paid, consumers are not left without recourse. The first year of operation confirmed its importance, with most eligible complaints arising from the investments and advice sector. Common causes included: Most complaints eligible for the CSLR were received from the investments and advice sector. See here for more details. In many matters assessed by AFCA, consumers were pressured into high‑risk investments, often at scale, against their best interests. These cases highlight the need for legal and regulatory changes and improved industry compliance with advice obligations, to prevent future consumer harm and reduce the overall number of claims made to the CSLR. In 2024‑25, we awarded $117 million in compensation to eligible CSLR complainants, with Dixon Advisory cases still the largest group of complaints we have ever managed. Complaints against insolvent financial firms remained high, making this the second‑biggest year on record. Our focus has been on assessing complaints funded by the initial top 10 industry levy. AFCA’s early engagement with the CSLR was driven by the large volume of Dixon Advisory complaints. Since then, nearly 50 financial firms have been linked to CSLR‑eligible matters, particularly in investments and advice. AFCA is committed to playing our part to ensure that the CSLR continues to provide meaningful protection for consumers and reinforces trust in Australia’s financial system. Insights from CSLR‑related complaints are already shaping regulatory reforms aimed at improving professional standards and practices, and reducing the number of future complaints. AFCA continues to work with Government, regulators and industry to ensure harmful models are identified early, and to encourage industry to improve practices and governance so that insolvencies and the resulting harm to consumers, can be reduced. title: Complaint allocation information
body: AFCA's progress on Shield and First Guardian complaints AFCA has issued lead decisions about the financial firms involved in the Shield and First Guardian collapse. Now that lead decisions have been issued for these firms, AFCA is able to progress other complaints in those groups more efficiently. Read more here Updated: 4 March 2026 Complaint allocation information for significant scale events If you’ve lodged a complaint with AFCA relating to a significant scale event, this page provides the latest updates on when complaint(s) may be assigned to a case worker. You’ll also find guidance on what you can do while waiting. We encourage you to bookmark this page and check it regularly for updated timeframes. How to check your allocation timeframe When you lodged your complaint, AFCA gave you a case number. You’ll find this number at the top of the email we sent you when your complaint was registered and in other correspondence we’ve sent you. It looks like this: 12-25-123456 Here’s what it means: Below, you’ll see case number ranges and expected timeframes by each financial firm involved in a significant scale event. For example: If your case number falls before 124000, we expect your complaint to be allocated by 31 March 2026. Case numbers go in order based on when complaints were lodged. Lower numbers mean earlier complaints. For example, 123000 was lodged with AFCA before 124000. Complaint allocation information by firm Select the financial firm relevant to your complaint to view estimated timeframes: If your case number is before 12-25-288854, we expect your complaint to be allocated by 30 July 2026. AFCA provided an update on Interprac complaints on 28 May 2028. Read this update here. If your case number is before 12-25-246446, we expect your complaint to be allocated by 30 July 2026 If your case number is before 12-25-361696, we expect your complaint to be allocated by 30 July 2026. If your case number is before 12-25-375082, we expect your complaint to be allocated by 30 July 2026. If your case number is before 12-25-236166, we expect your complaint to be allocated by 30 July 2026. Note: These timeframes are estimates only and may change as we move through complaints. Please check these pages regularly for updates. 12 – Financial complaint (the type of complaint) 25 – Year lodged (2025, 2026, etc.) 123456 – Your unique case number (numbers increase as complaints are lodged) What to expect next When your complaint is allocated to a case worker, they will contact you directly to discuss next steps and any additional information required. What you can do while you wait Gather documentation: Providing documents such as advice statements, transaction records, and correspondence can help us investigate your complaint more efficiently once it is allocated. See examples of the documents to provide here. If your complaint relates to the Shield and First Guardian collapse: Watch our free webinar here to learn more about AFCA and our process. Complete o

title: Complaint discussion
body: At AFCA, we provide consumers and small businesses with fair, free and independent dispute resolution for financial complaints. We noticed you began lodging a complaint through our online portal and would like to invite you to join a confidential discussion to understand why it wasn’t completed, and to gain any suggestions for improvement. You can view our privacy policy here. We’ve partnered with research firm Strativity to conduct these discussions on AFCA’s behalf, which will be held online via Zoom over the next two weeks. Sessions will last approximately 60-90 minutes, and you’ll receive an online prepaid Visa gift card worth $200 for participating.

title: Complaint preparation checklist
body: Your investment journey You do not need to complete the entire form to make a complaint. You also do not need to complete this form before making your complaint. You can complete this form after making a complaint to help AFCA understand your complaint in more detail. This checklist is designed to help you reflect on your personal investment journey and prepare to lodge a complaint with AFCA. Investment matters can be complex, and often involve multiple parties, such as advisers, brokers, trustees, and platform providers, each playing a different role. By completing this form, you’ll be able to: You can select more than one option in each section if multiple parties or steps apply to your situation. There is also space to add your own notes and details. Once completed, this form can help you: Please note, this form is not a complaint form. You will need to submit a complaint to AFCA using our online complaint form, by email or phone. You should attach this form as part of your complaint submission along with other supporting documents. If you need help completing this form, you can contact AFCA at info@afca.org.au or call 1800 931 678. Click here to download the checklist Information pack: examples of documents to provide with your complaint Investment journey checklist Identify the key people and organisations involved in your investment experience Clarify what each party said or did, and how they influenced your decisions Gather the documents and information AFCA may need to assess your complaint Understand who to lodge your complaint against Submit a clear and complete complaint (or multiple complaints) to AFCA Resources Factsheet: information when you have a complaint about an insolvent firm Factsheet: class actions affecting AFCA complaints Factsheet: how we resolve complaints and our Rules Factsheet: SMSF complaints Factsheet: managing financial advice AFCA Approach: determining compensation in complaints against Financial Advice Firms where the Responsible Entity of a Managed Investment Scheme has become insolvent Question and answers

title: Complaints dating back to 1 January 2008
body: 25 February 2019 Following the Royal Commission’s Final Report, the Government announced that AFCA is to consider complaints dating back to 1 January 2008, if the complaint otherwise falls within AFCA’s current thresholds. The Government has announced that AFCA will consider eligible complaints between 1 July 2019 and 30 June 2020. AFCA will run a limited consultation regarding required changes to its Rules, which will need to be approved by the Australian Securities and Investments Commission. We have provided advice on our website to let consumers and small business owners who have legacy disputes dating back to 1 January 2008 know that we will shortly be issuing guidance about what this means for them; including how and when they can lodge a complaint with us. Complaints that meet the extended timeframe You may now be receiving enquiries from customers about complaints dating back to 1 January 2008. We encourage you to resolve these matters through internal dispute resolution process (IDR) processes. If a complaint is lodged with us, it is likely we will refer the complaint back to the financial firm. If you receive enquiries from consumers or small businesses about disputes dating back to 1 January 2008, we encourage you to review the issue and attempt to resolve the matter through your firm’s IDR. If a complaint is lodged with us and we find that the matter has not undergone your firm’s IDR process, it is likely the complaint will be referred back to your firm. We may ask firms for consent to consider matters that are currently lodged which meet the extended timeframe criteria, ahead of the formal Rules change to allow us to accept complaints dating from 1 January 2008. Additionally, if you have previously been referred a complaint which falls within the extended timeframe and have raised a case action to dispute the jurisdiction, we suggest that you may consider rethinking your previous objection. Similarly, where part of a dispute may fall into this extended timeframe, we suggest you consider rethinking an objection. We will ask for your consent before 1 July 2019 We may ask our members for consent to consider current complaints which meet the extended timeframe criteria (ahead of 1 July 2019). If you have previously been referred a complaint which falls within the extended timeframe and have requested a jurisdictional review, we also ask you to reconsider the request. Similarly, where part of a complaint may fall into this extended timeframe, we also suggest you reconsider the objection. A new action “Provide consent to AFCA to consider complaint” has been added to Secure Services, making this process available immediately. Please contact our membership team on membership@afca.org.au or call 1300 56 55 62 if you have any questions.

title: Complaints we consider
description: Discover the types of complaints AFCA considers, including financial disputes related to banking, insurance, superannuation, and more. Click here to see how AFCA can help.
body: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) considers a wide range of complaints from consumers and small businesses about financial firms. To make a complaint to us: Find out more about the types of complaints we can consider: You must be eligible to complain The complaint needs to be about a financial firm that is an AFCA member. The complaint must meet our threshold requirements (such as being lodged within time) and be within our monetary jurisdiction. Your complaint cannot be excluded by our Rules. Credit, loans and finance Insurance Banking payments and transactions Investments and financial advice Superannuation More information Our jurisdiction Our Decision Makers Search for an AFCA member Codes of practice Am I eligible? Our complaint resolution service is available to the following: We can also consider a complaint from: consumers, who we define as being an individual small businesses of less than 100 employees whether a primary production business or otherwise, which includes: a sole trader a partnership an incorporated business. a sole trader a partnership an incorporated business. a registered charity, regardless of the number of employees a club that carries on a business and has less than 100 employees). Who are AFCA members? Some of the organisations that are required by legislation to be AFCA members are: We refer to these organisations as 'financial firms'. Many Australian financial services licensees, Australian credit licensees, authorised credit representatives and superannuation trustees are required to be a member of the Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) under their licence conditions. Other firms have joined AFCA voluntarily as part of a commitment to accountability in their dispute resolution. Our members include banks, insurers, credit providers, financial advisers, debt collection agencies, superannuation trustees and many more. Search for a financial firm Disclaimer A financial firm’s membership of AFCA is not an endorsement of that firm or the services it provides. AFCA does not guarantee or make any recommendation, representation or warranty with respect to the firm or its services. Information about financial firms may be available via https://asicconnect.asic.gov.au/. banks and other credit providers financial planning firms general insurers insurance broking firms life insurers superannuation fund trustees, retirement savings account (RSA) providers stock broking firms fund management companies What types of complaints can’t we consider? In summary we can’t consider the following complaints: However, there are complaints about fees, premiums, charges, rebates or interest rates that we can consider, including where someone believes that a fee or other charge has not been disclosed, has been misrepresented or has not been calculated correctly. Our Rules and Operational Guidelines provide more detailed information about these exclusions (and others) in section C. We cannot generally consider a

title: Conciliation resolution rate
body: The conciliation resolution rate is one measure of the effectiveness of conciliation to resolve complaints. What does it show? The conciliation resolution rate shows how many complaints resolved as a result of a conciliation conference. A high conciliation resolution rate shows a financial firm is able to resolve disputes that reach this stage without a formal decision by AFCA. A low conciliation resolution rate shows that a conciliation is not currently an effective way to resolve complaints against a particular firm and there may be opportunities to introduce best practice and improve how they engage with conciliations. How the conciliation resolution rate is calculated The conciliation resolution rate is calculated using the following formula: Please note that data about conciliation conferences conducted by phone can take up to a month to appear on the dashboard. Complaints the AFCA case manager has marked as closed due to conciliation, as a percentage of total conciliations conducted during the reporting period. Best practice During a conciliation conference, an AFCA conciliator acts as a facilitator between a financial firm and a complainant, guiding discussions to find a fair agreement. Here are some steps financial firms can take to prepare for and during a conciliation conference. Before the conciliation conference: Consider the complaint from your point of view. Come with an understanding of the key issues of the complaint, and your/the firm’s response to the dispute. Consider the complaint from the complainant’s point of view. This will help you understand their unique circumstances, and how they might approach the conciliation. Consider the complaint from AFCA’s point of view. This will help understand what will happen if the complaint does not resolve on the day. Have relevant correspondence and documents on hand to refer to and share with AFCA and the complainant. Make sure you have the authority to resolve the complaint on the day. If that is not possible, make sure to have a contact available in your firm who can authorise resolution on the day. If you have not done so, read the attending a conciliation conference fact sheet, or watching the conciliation webcast on our website. During the conciliation conference: We will ask the complainant to summarise their complaint first. We will then ask you to outline your firm’s response in a clear way. We will sometimes ask questions that we already know the answer to. We do this to get the parties talking about what needs to be discussed when the time is right. Approach the conference call in good faith and with an open mind to what the issues are and what a potential resolution could look like. Try to be clear and concise, and avoid industry jargon. If required, take notes during the conciliation. Remember, you can’t prepare for everything. New information is presented all the time, existing information does not get exchanged, and questions will be asked that you were not prepared for. I

title: ​​Consultation on AFCA’s approach to claims for non-financial loss​
description: Understand AFCA's methodology for handling non-financial loss claims, aimed at providing comprehensive resolutions for affected consumers.
body: Thank you for taking the time to provide feedback on our consultation. This consultation is now closed. We will be reviewing feedback and submissions over the coming weeks, and will incorporate any necessary amendments to the approach. About AFCA’s approach to claims for non-financial loss AFCA’s Approach to claims for non-financial loss is not new. It sets out AFCA’s approach to assessing these claims by outlining: the circumstances in which a claim for non-financial loss can arise AFCA’s expectations of a complainant in dealing with a problem limits on awards of compensation principles applied in awarding compensation. Proposed amendments AFCA’s current Approach to claims for non-financial loss includes case studies, analysis and examples of compensation that was awarded in particular circumstances. However, the current Approach does not include an objective scale to help parties understand how AFCA might determine the appropriate amount of non-financial loss to be awarded. To clarify this and improve transparency, AFCA is proposing to add a matrix as a guide on what might be an appropriate range for an award of compensation for non-financial loss. The matrix explains how much compensation AFCA might choose to award based on the impact of the firm’s conduct on the complainant, and duration of that impact. AFCA has also updated its case studies and reviewed the Approach to clarify its jurisdiction and ensure consistency of language. AFCA's current Approach to non-financial loss claims (last updated October 2022) AFCA's proposed amended Approach to non-financial loss claims (March 2023) About the consultation Consultation on AFCA’s Approach to claims for non-financial loss is taking place from 3 April to 1 May 2023. Although the proposed changes to the Approach are not substantial, AFCA wishes to consult with external stakeholders to ensure there are no unintended consequences as a result of the changes. AFCA’s consultation process has been designed to be flexible and accessible. This means, stakeholders can provide feedback, comments and questions in meetings with AFCA people, in writing (via email and via post), or via a formal submission. Making a submission To share your feedback, comments, questions or submission, you can: Email: approaches@afca.org.au If you have a formal submission document, please attach this to your email. Write to: Senior Consultant – Approach Frameworks Australian Financial Complaints Authority GPO Box 3 Melbourne VIC 3001 Contact us To contact us about AFCA’s Approach to claims for non-financial loss or if you have questions about the consultation, please email approaches@afca.org.au. Frequently Asked Questions What is an AFCA Approach document? AFCA’s Approach documents help consumers and financial firms better understand how we reach decisions about key issues. These documents explain the way AFCA might approach some common issues and complaint types. What is compensation for non-financial loss? Compensation for non

title: Consultation on AFCA's general insurance claims handling approach
body: Consultation closed Consultation on AFCA’s Approach to general insurance claims handling has now closed. The consultation took place between Monday 28 July to Friday 29 August 2025. AFCA would like to thank stakeholders for their participation in the consultation process. We are now considering formal submissions and how we can best respond to the feedback we received. We will publish an AFCA Consultation Response Paper with the final version of the new Approach document, alongside all non-confidential submissions, on this page in the coming months. About the consultation AFCA Approach documents support consumers, small businesses and financial firms’ understanding of AFCA’s approach to certain issues and how we reach our decisions. The Approach documents provide practical information on how AFCA will assess and determine complaints and enhance AFCA’s transparency, consistency, and efficiency. They outline how AFCA applies legal principles, industry codes, regulatory guidance and considers good industry practice when investigating and handling complaints. These documents are informed by feedback from our stakeholders, including government, regulators, financial firms, and consumer advocates. AFCA is consulting on a new Approach to general insurance claims handling. This Approach document sets out how AFCA considers various types of complaints relating to the handling of general insurance claims, against an insurer’s legal obligations, and has regard to industry standards. By participating in this consultation, stakeholders can contribute to creating more predictable and efficient resolution processes, which in turn can reduce the time and resources needed to handle complaints. Approach document and Consultation Paper Read AFCA’s updated Approach to general insurance claims handling. Please provide your feedback through one of the channels below. The Consultation Paper explains the purpose of the Approach document and supports stakeholders in their submission. Consultation key dates Timeline Date Milestone Monday 28 July 2025 Consultation on AFCA’s Approach to general insurance claims handling opens Friday 29 August 2025 Consultation on AFCA’s Approach to general insurance claims handling closes Sept-Nov 2025 AFCA to consider and respond to consultation submissions and feedback Late 2025 AFCA publishes final Approach document on general insurance claims handling, submissions and consultation report Contact us If you have any questions about the draft Approach documents or the consultation process, please email consultation@afca.org.au. Related links FAQs

title: Consultation on AFCA’s Superannuation Approaches
body: Consultations closed Update: AFCA has published three updated superannuation Approaches following extensive consultations with industry, regulators and consumer advocates. Delivered as part of our FY24-25 Approaches Consultation schedule, these new and amended documents clarify AFCA’s approach on a range of complex matters, including: AFCA would like to thank stakeholders for a constructive and cooperative consultation process. Formal submissions are published on the consultation page below. Formal submissions AFCA has now published the final Approach documents, and separate consultation feedback reports, outlining how we responded to formal submissions and stakeholder feedback. Delayed insurance claims in superannuation Sections 29(6) and (7) of the Insurance Contracts Act Superannuation death benefits About the consultations Our Approaches are developed and updated to provide greater clarity around what to expect from AFCA processes, greater transparency about the way we investigate complaints and make decisions, and ensure we continue to deliver consistent outcomes for complaints to AFCA. The documents will help complainants, financial firms and stakeholders understand how AFCA considers complaints in the two key areas of relation to delayed insurance claims in superannuation, sections 29(6) and (7) of the Insurance Contracts Act and superannuation death benefits. They outline how AFCA applies legal principles, industry codes, regulatory guidance and considers good industry practice when investigating these complaints. The documents are informed by feedback received during our complaint handling process with our stakeholders, including government, regulators, financial firms and consumer advocates. Together, our updated Approaches are designed to help trustees, members, and their advocates understand what to expect from AFCA’s process. They support faster, more consistent outcomes and reinforce AFCA’s commitment to a free, fair, and independent external dispute resolution scheme. Consultation feedback report on AFCA’s updated Approach to Superannuation death benefits AFCA’s updated approach to Superannuation death benefits includes important guidance for trustees in cases involving family violence. This guidance recognises strong community concerns about the risk of superannuation entitlements going to perpetrators of abuse and the profound harm this can entail. In response to valuable stakeholder feedback, we have updated our Approach document to: The AFCA Approach to Superannuation Death Benefit Complaints Read the consultation report here Emphasise that trustees must consider each death benefit claim on its own facts to ensure fair and reasonable outcomes. Clarify that the primary purpose is to provide for dependants who would have relied on the member for financial support. Include information on the steps trustees should take once notified of a member's death. Expand information on the scope and implications of binding and non-lapsing nom

title: Consultation on amendments to Rule C.1.4b) and d)
body: Summary of proposed changes Recent legislative change has introduced a ban on life insurers soliciting or using protected genetic information when offering life insurance. As a result, AFCA is consulting on proposed changes to its Rules which will enable AFCA to deal with complaints arising from an insurer’s compliance with the ban. To consider complaints about the solicitation or use of adverse genetic testing results, AFCA needs to amend both Rule C.1.4b) and d). Subject to consultation and regulatory approval, these amendments are expected to take effect from 8 October 2026. Background On 8 April 2026 the Treasury Laws Amendment (Genetic Testing Protections in Life Insurance and Other Measures) Act was passed, and it introduced a ban on life insurers soliciting or using protected genetic information when offering life insurance. The ban comes into force from 8 October 2026. Proposed Rules amendments Rule C.1.4b) Current Rule C.1.4b) C.1.4 AFCA must exclude: … b) A complaint about underwriting or actuarial factors leading to an offer of a Life Insurance Policy on non-standard terms. Proposed new Rule C.1.4b) C.1.4 AFCA must exclude: … b) A complaint about underwriting or actuarial factors leading to an offer of a Life Insurance Policy on non-standard terms unless the complaint relates to an allegation of the solicitation or use of protected genetic information by the life insurer [contrary to section 33H of the Insurance Contracts Act 1984. Rule C.1.4d) Current Rule C.1.4d) C.1.4 AFCA must exclude: … d) A complaint about a decision to refuse to provide insurance cover except where (i) the complaint is that the decision was made indiscriminately, maliciously or on the basis of incorrect information; (ii) the complaint is that the Complainant was misinformed about the insurance cover; or (iii) the complaint relates to a Medical Indemnity Insurance Product. For the avoidance of doubt, rules C.1.4 (b), (d)(i) and (d)(ii) apply to a Superannuation Complaint to the extent that the insurance policy is issued to either the trustee of a Regulated Superannuation Fund, or an Approved Deposit Fund or an RSA provider. Proposed new Rule C.1.4d) C.1.4 AFCA must exclude: … d) A complaint about a decision to refuse to provide insurance cover except where (i) the complaint is that the decision was made indiscriminately, maliciously or on the basis of incorrect information; (ii) the complaint is that the Complainant was misinformed about the insurance cover; (iii) the complaint relates to a Medical Indemnity Insurance Product; or (iv) the complaint relates to an allegation of the soliciting or use of protected genetic information by a life insurer [contrary to section 33H of the Insurance Contracts Act 1984 (Cth) or section 46(3) of the Disability Discrimination Act 1992 (Cth)]. For the avoidance of doubt, rules C.1.4 (b), (d)(i) and (d)(ii) apply to a Superannuation Complaint to the extent that the insurance policy is issued to either the trustee of a Regulated

title: Consultation on draft investments and advice Approach document
description: Review AFCA's approach to determining compensation for complaints involving financial advisers and managed investment schemes, ensuring fair outcomes for all parties.
body: Consultation on AFCA’s draft Approach to determining compensation in complaints against Financial Advice Firms where the Responsible Entity of a Managed Investment Scheme has become insolvent, is now closed. The public consultation ran from Monday 6 November to Friday 1 December 2023. AFCA would like to thank stakeholders for a constructive and cooperative consultation process. AFCA has now published the final Approach document and consultation feedback report, outlining how we responded to formal submissions and stakeholder feedback. About the Approach AFCA’s Approach documents help complainants and financial firms better understand how AFCA might approach some common issues and complaint types, and how we reach decisions. The AFCA Approach to determining compensation in complaints against Financial Advice Firms where the Responsible Entity of a Managed Investment Scheme has become insolvent complements the existing AFCA Approach to calculating financial loss in financial advice complaints, and was drafted in response to requests from stakeholders in the context of the passing of legislation for a Compensation Scheme of Last Resort (CSLR). The purpose of the additional Approach document is to clarify for financial firms and complainants how AFCA approaches liability and loss when a financial advice firm is found to have breached its obligations to the complainant in circumstances where the RE of a MIS has become insolvent. The final Approach document and consultation feedback report The AFCA Approach to determining compensation in complaints against Financial Advice Firms where the Responsible Entity of a Managed Investment Scheme has become insolvent has now been published. We have also published a consultation feedback report outlining how we responded to formal submissions and stakeholder feedback. Read more Formal submissions We received a total of five formal written submissions, including four non-confidential submissions. Read more Related links Consultations Investments and financial advice complaints AFCA Approaches About AFCA AFCA’s Rules

title: Consultation on new approaches
description: Explore new approaches in AFCA's dispute resolution process, designed to improve fairness and efficiency in handling financial complaints.
body: Consultation closed Consultation on AFCA’s draft Approaches to Responsible Lending and Lending to Small Business (formerly Appropriate Lending to Small Business) have now closed. The consultation periods were: AFCA would like to thank stakeholders for a constructive and cooperative consultation process. Formal submissions are published on the consultation pages below. AFCA’s Responsible Lending Approach – 31 July to 11 September 2023 AFCA’s Lending to Small Business Approach – 18 August to 29 September 2023 About the consultation The draft approaches have been developed to provide greater clarity around what to expect from AFCA processes, greater transparency about the way we investigate complaints and make decisions, and ensure we continue to deliver consistent outcomes for complaints to AFCA. The documents will help complainants, financial firms and stakeholders understand how AFCA considers complaints in the two key areas of Responsible Lending and Lending to Small Business. The Responsible Lending Approach explains how we consider these complaints, including: Read more how we assess a financial firm’s compliance with responsible lending obligations how we determine a fair outcome where a firm breaches its responsible lending obligations, and how we calculate loss and assess benefits to determine compensation.
