title: ​​Diversity, Inclusion and Belonging​
description: Learn about AFCA's commitment to diversity, inclusion, and belonging. Explore initiatives that ensure a supportive and respectful work environment where all employees can thrive, feel valued, and contribute to AFCA’s success.
body: At AFCA, we value different backgrounds and encourage distinct perspectives. We see and hear people for who they are; we feel safe, respected, and we’re able to be our authentic selves. We use this approach to deliver better outcomes for the customers and communities we serve. Diversity Inclusion Belonging At AFCA, we have a broad view of diversity that includes all the ways all people are different. We extend this definition to go beneath what’s visible to include all the deeper ways we are diverse including values, experiences, identities, perspectives, expertise, knowledge and ideas that make up AFCA. These differences come together to benefit all. Belonging is the feeling of security and support when there is a sense of acceptance, inclusion, and identity for employees. Employees that feel as though they belong, feel safe to speak up, supported, inspired, confident and empowered to do their best work and to learn and grow. Diversity At AFCA, we have a broad view of diversity that includes all the ways all people are different. We extend this definition to go beneath what’s visible to include all the deeper ways we are diverse including values, experiences, identities, perspectives, expertise, knowledge and ideas that make up AFCA. These differences come together to benefit all. Inclusion Belonging Belonging is the feeling of security and support when there is a sense of acceptance, inclusion, and identity for employees. Employees that feel as though they belong, feel safe to speak up, supported, inspired, confident and empowered to do their best work and to learn and grow. Our Diversity, Inclusion and Belonging Strategy When we launched our first Diversity Inclusion and Belonging strategy and action plan in 2023, we set out with ambition and energy to build a more inclusive organisation. Our renewed 2026-27 action plan reflects the maturity of our approach. Where our first action plan focused on building infrastructure and creating awareness, this plan is about deepening impact, with key activities across: Accountability and governance Inclusive systems and process Inclusive leadership Diverse and inclusive teams Read our Diversity, Inclusion and Belonging Strategy “When our people feel seen, respected, and able to be their authentic selves, they show up differently – more engaged, more connected to our purpose, and better equipped to deliver fair and equitable outcomes for the people who rely on our service.” David Locke, Chief Ombudsman and CEO “Our people are at the heart of everything we do at AFCA. I am committed to understanding the work and action required to genuinely achieve diversity at AFCA, where all our people are included, and feel like they belong.” Dr June Smith, Deputy Chief Ombudsman This Strategy and Action Plan complements our Reflect Reconciliation Action Plan, which affirms our commitment to reconciliation with First Nations peoples, our people, and our wider stakeholder network; and our Accessibility Strategy, which ens

title: Dixon Advisory and Superannuation Services Pty Ltd (Subject to Deed of Company Arrangement)
description: AFCA has been advised that Dixon Advisory and Superannuation Services Pty Ltd (Subject to Deed of Company Arrangement) (Dixon) has been placed into Voluntary Administration.
body: Dixon Advisory update – July 2024 Dixon Advisory & Superannuation Services Pty Ltd (Subject to Deed of Company arrangement) was expelled from the Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) on 30 June 2024. AFCA cannot accept any new complaints about Dixon Advisory. This is because AFCA can only accept complaints about firms that are current members of the AFCA scheme. The expulsion of Dixon Advisory does not prevent AFCA from considering and finalising complaints received on or before 29 June 2024. Work is well under way, but it will take time to get through the large number of Dixon complaints. We thank people for their patience. It is important that this work is conducted with the usual rigour that’s required. Details of administrators When a firm is in an insolvency process it is important that any communications or queries about the process are directed to those now responsible for the firm. The administrators for Dixon Advisory are Stephen Longley and Craig Crosbie of PwC Partners. They can be contacted at: au_dass_queries@pwc.com On 26 August 2024 the administrators for Dixon Advisory and Superannuation Services Pty Ltd (Subject to Deed of Company arrangement) issued a Notice of Intention to Declare a Dividend to former clients of Dixon Advisory who they consider are creditors under the Deed of Company Arrangement. If you have any questions regarding the communication from the administrators of Dixon Advisory, please contact them at au_dass_queries@pwc.com. This activity is separate to AFCA’s focus of investigating and resolving open Dixon complaints. The action of declaring and distributing a dividend will not impact AFCA’s consideration of open complaints. However, if AFCA determines that there was inappropriate personal finance product advice by a former advisor of Dixon which resulted in loss to the consumer, the award of any compensation will consider any distribution received from the administration. Complaints received As at 30 June 2024, AFCA has registered 2,773 complaints against Dixon Advisory since AFCA’s inception at 1 November 2018. Paused complaints are under investigation In January 2022, AFCA was advised that Dixon Advisory had been placed into Voluntary Administration. As a result, AFCA paused progress of all complaints against Dixon Advisory, pending the establishment of the Compensation Scheme of Last Resort on 2 April 2024. AFCA is currently investigating these complaints and is assessing if they may be eligible to raise a CSLR claim. Whether or not a former client of Dixon Advisory is eligible to raise a claim for compensation will depend on the individual circumstances of the advice they were given, the scope of the CSLR as set out in the legislation, as well as AFCA’s jurisdiction to consider the complaint. If a complaint falls within AFCA’s jurisdiction and is otherwise likely to be eligible for compensation under the CSLR, AFCA will consider the complaint further. If the complaint is not eligible, AFCA will c

title: Efficiency and effectiveness
description: Discover how AFCA ensures efficiency and effectiveness in handling financial disputes, focusing on continuous improvement and stakeholder satisfaction.
body: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) is required by legislation to operate in a way that is accessible, independent, fair, accountable, efficient and effective. The efficiency requirement recognises the importance of complaints being resolved in a timely way. We have designed a flexible complaints resolution process to cater for the range of complaints that we consider. To maximise our effectiveness, we consult with stakeholders, including consumer representatives and financial firms, and we aim to continually improve our performance. We consult and engage with stakeholders in a range of ways. These include industry forums and liaison meetings, consumer liaison group meetings, meetings with individual financial firms and consumer groups, and through the regular publications we release. We recognise repeat complaint issues and work with financial firms to resolve the underlying causes of these issues. We also support regulators by providing information to them about these issues. AFCA provides an efficient and effective service through a number of means, including: having compensation limits that are consistent with regulatory requirements, and exclusions that are appropriately limited in their scope having clear and appropriate time limits for complaints to be lodged with us following a clear and robust complaints resolution process having provisions in our Rules and Constitution that set out the consequences for financial firms if they don't comply with our Rules. This includes reporting any non-compliance or other serious breach to ASIC or another appropriate regulator resolving complaints in a timely way, and having standards that are monitored and reported internally to ASIC and publicly through our Annual Review.

title: El asesor independiente
body: El asesor independiente examina quejas relacionadas con los estándares de servicio brindado por la AFCA y opera de forma independiente al proceso de quejas internas de la AFCA. El asesor independiente no tiene autoridad para examinar los méritos o bases sobre las cuales la AFCA toma alguna decisión relacionada con alguna queja ante una compañía financiera. Una vez que el asesor independiente ha examinado y evaluado una queja sobre el servicio de la AFCA, no es posible apelar sus hallazgos o recomendaciones. ¿Quién es el asesor independiente? El asesor independiente es asignado por, y le reporta a la junta de la AFCA, y trabaja según los términos de referencia del asesor independiente. El asesor independiente no forma parte del funcionamiento diario de la AFCA, y no le reporta a la alta gerencia de la AFCA o al defensor principal del pueblo. La asesora independiente actual de la AFCA se llama Melissa Dwyer. Melissa cuenta con más de 20 años de experiencia como ejecutiva de algo rango en los sectores privados, públicos y sin fines de lucro, especializándose en dirección de empresas, auditoría interna y reforma e integridad. Fue la auditora ejecutiva principal del “Departamento de educación y capacitación de Victoria” y arquitecta del programa de reforma e integridad subsiguiente a las investigaciones realizadas por la “Comisión Independiente Anticorrupción de Base Amplia (IBAC).” ¿Quién puede quejarse ante el asesor independiente? Cualquier persona o negocio directamente afectado por la forma en que la AFCA ha manejado o está manejando una queja ante una compañía financiera puede quejarse ante el asesor independiente, incluyendo: reclamantes compañías financieras representantes grupos unidos. Quejas que puede evaluar el asesor independiente El asesor independiente puede evaluar quejas relacionadas con el estándar de servicio brindado por la AFCA al manejar una queja, incluyendo pero sin limitarse a quejas sobre: el profesionalismo, competencia y actitud del personal comunicación justicia e imparcialidad puntualidad lealtad a los procesos de la AFCA. Quejas que el asesor independiente no puede tomar en consideración El asesor independiente no puede tomar en consideración quejas relacionadas con: las acciones o falta de acción por parte de una compañía financiera o el reclamante las decisiones o hallazgos de la AFCA, incluyendo evaluaciones preliminares, determinaciones y decisiones jurisdiccionales el manejo de algún problema sistemático por parte de la AFCA las cuotas que la AFCA cobra a compañías financieras. El asesor independiente no puede cambiar la resolución de una decisión de la AFCA El asesor independiente no puede cambiar o anular una determinación de la AFCA o decisión jurisdiccional en relación a una queja ante una compañía financiera. No es una apelación o mecanismo de revisión de una decisión, o juicio de los hechos o méritos en los que se basa una queja ante una compañía financiera. Resultados que pueden obtenerse del asesor independi

title: Employee entitlement schemes
body: ASIC has updated guidance on the regulation of employee entitlement schemes with the release of Information Sheet 295 (INFO 295). As part of their licensing obligations, employee entitlement schemes are required to become a member of AFCA by 1 September 2026. This page brings together essential resources and information specifically for employee entitlement schemes and their consumers. (We note that employee entitlement schemes may refer to their consumers as, workers, members, employer members, customers or participants). For consumers Please note: If an employee entitlement scheme is not yet a member of AFCA, we cannot consider complaints against them. Consumers experiencing issues with an employee entitlement scheme should first try to resolve their complaint directly with their operator. Consumers who utilise employee entitlement schemes will now have access to free, fair, and independent external dispute resolution through AFCA, if they are unable to resolve complaints directly with their operator. Once an employee entitlement scheme becomes a member of AFCA, they will be listed on our financial firm search. For help, consumers can contact AFCA by emailing info@afca.org.au or calling 1800 931 678 (free call). For employee entitlement scheme operators The updated guidance in INFO 295 outlines important protections for consumers of employee entitlement schemes, including access to external dispute resolution. AFCA is committed to supporting new members with these changes. We appreciate new member firms may be unsure of AFCA’s role as an external dispute resolution provider. To support new members of AFCA, we have developed resources to assist employee entitlement schemes with this transition to becoming an AFCA member. If you are looking to become a member of AFCA, or are unfamiliar with AFCA, our dedicated Employee Entitlement Scheme new member guide can help you understand and navigate AFCA’s complaint resolution process, jurisdiction and approach. It is important to note AFCA’s Fee Structure and how fees and charges work for the current period and new financial year beginning 1 July 2026. If your organisation has questions about becoming an AFCA member, please email our membership team at membership@afca.org.au or call 1300 56 55 62.

title: Employee Resource Groups
body: Careers Home Why AFCA Our Teams Joining Us Careers News Feel proud to belong in our Employee Resource Groups Our Employee Resource Groups (ERGs) are the pillars of our inclusive culture. Dedicated, diverse, and proactive, they take pride in fostering a supportive and engaging environment for all employees. Every day, our ERGs work together to promote best practices in inclusion and belonging, raise awareness, create positive change, and connect with various diverse communities across AFCA. “I know I can proudly be myself at work and openly support others. I highly value how inclusive AFCA is.” - Raina, Business Operations Analyst Ally Network The Ally Network celebrates and supports AFCA’s LGBTQIA+ employees and allies by hosting social events to promote and celebrate important LGBTQIA+ milestones throughout the year, educating staff on and answering questions about LGBTQIA+ related issues. It also provides support to our LGBTQIA+ employees and allies and promotes a safe and inclusive workplace for all AFCA employees. Vis-Ability Network The Vis-Ability Network is dedicated to promoting diversity, inclusion, and belonging at AFCA by advocating with and supporting employees who identify as having a disability, or care for a person who does. Our goal is to create a workplace where everybody belongs, and our unique differences, including disability and neurodiversity, are welcomed and celebrated. Carer’s Network The Carer's Network is dedicated to promoting diversity, inclusion, and belonging at AFCA by advocating for and supporting employees who are carers. This achievement acknowledges AFCA’s work and commitment to delivering genuine inclusion and accessibility for all our people. MOSAIC This achievement acknowledges AFCA’s work and commitment to delivering genuine inclusion and accessibility for all our people. The Women’s Network The Women's Network at AFCA aims to foster connection and support and drive collective action with and for anyone identifying as women. All AFCA staff are welcome to join to collectively achieve our core purpose. This achievement acknowledges AFCA’s work and commitment to delivering genuine inclusion and accessibility for all our people. "AFCA fosters opportunities for individuals to excel in their work and careers while enabling them to be their true, authentic selves in the workplace." - John, Head of Membership Services (Acting) Read more Learn more about what it feels like to be part of our ERGs What does a role in our this team entail? Watch these videos for a sneak peak into some of the responsibilities and challenges Careers news Discover our latest updates, insights and stories highlighting how AFCA employees are making a meaningful impact in their careers and shaping our industry.

title: Enabling legislation
description: Enabling legislation
body: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) scheme was authorised by the Minister for Revenue and Financial Services, under the Treasury Laws Amendment (Putting Consumers First—Establishment of the Australian Financial Complaints Authority) Act 2018 (Cth) (the AFCA Act). The AFCA Act gained Royal Assent on 5 March 2018. The AFCA Act sets out standards that AFCA must meet; it provides additional powers in relation to superannuation disputes and gives ASIC certain regulatory powers, including the power to issue directions. AFCA’s terms of reference will set out how legislative and policy requirements will be met. The AFCA Act amended the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) and other related legislation, to implement the central recommendations of the Ramsay Review for: The AFCA Act outlines how the AFCA external dispute resolution scheme should operate, including authorisation of the scheme, and mandatory requirements and general considerations for the operation and regulation of AFCA. The AFCA Act also repealed the Superannuation (Resolution of Complaints) Act 1993 (Cth) and gives AFCA additional provisions relating to superannuation. Under the AFCA Act, AFCA is required to report to ASIC, APRA and the ATO about certain matters, and ASIC has power to issue certain directions to AFCA. The AFCA Act also outlines the general considerations for an external dispute resolution scheme, which apply to AFCA as part of its authorisation. These are: the establishment of a new one-stop-shop external dispute resolution (EDR) framework – the Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) – replacing the Superannuation Complaints Tribunal (SCT) and the existing EDR schemes approved by the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC); the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS); and the Credit and Investments Ombudsman (CIO) an enhanced internal dispute resolution (IDR) framework to deal with all consumer financial disputes about products and services provided by financial firms, including superannuation disputes. the accessibility of the scheme the independence of the scheme the fairness of the scheme the accountability of the scheme the efficiency of the scheme the effectiveness of the scheme. Related information AFCA's Rules AFCA's Operational Guidelines Corporate information AFCA Board Senior Leadership Group CEO and Chief Ombudsman

title: Engagement Charter
description: Learn about AFCA's Engagement Charter, outlining the expected conduct and responsibilities of financial firms, complainants, and AFCA employees to ensure fair and respectful dispute resolution.
body: AFCA has developed an Engagement Charter to summarise what it expects from all users of its service, including AFCA. The AFCA Engagement Charter shares AFCA’s values and outlines the behaviour it expects from financial firms, complainants and AFCA employees when resolving disputes. It also outlines AFCA’s obligations to be fair, independent and impartial, as stated in AFCA’s Rules. The Charter is not a new set of guidelines, it is instead a living document that makes the roles, responsibilities and expectations of each party more explicit so that AFCA’s stakeholders have a shared understanding of good conduct. Key to the Charter is AFCA’s expectation that all parties cooperate reasonably with the common goal of bringing finality to a complaint. It says all parties should engage with each other and AFCA in a way that is transparent and honest, respectful and fair, in good faith, and efficient and cooperative. The Charter also sets out how AFCA will respond to financial firms and complainants that fail to comply with its expectations when resolving a dispute. As stated in the ACFA Rules, it can, at its discretion, stop engaging with a party in exceptional circumstances. The Charter was developed after consultation with our stakeholders and we thank them for their feedback. Some of the high level themes from our consultation feedback included the need for AFCA to provide transparency around processes which underpin the principles in the Charter, clarity around roles and responsibilities and publication of reporting data. AFCA is addressing this important feedback and undertaking improvements to our service. AFCA will call out and escalate behaviour it considers inconsistent with the principles in the Charter and will act against parties that fail to meet its standards. Contents Download the PDF Purpose Expectations for engagement What you can expect from us Expectations for financial firms Expectations for complainants Expectations for representatives We will not tolerate unreasonable behaviour Meeting the expectations in this Charter

title: Equity of Access Program
body: Annual Review 2024-25 Download the PDF Previous Annual Reviews Contents Everyone deserves a fair chance at having their complaint resolved, but not everyone comes to us with the same background, capacity, resilience or resources. While many people navigate our service with ease, others face significant barriers such as language, disability, financial stress or low literacy. For these consumers, the process can feel daunting, confusing or out of reach. The Equity of Access Program is about removing those barriers so every person has the same opportunity for a fair process and a fair outcome. This is not about creating more rules or complexity; it is about rethinking how we deliver our service so that people feel seen, supported and are able to participate fully in their complaint. Designing with equity in mind improves the experience for everyone. When the process is easier to understand for the most vulnerable, it becomes clearer, faster and more efficient for all. Equity is not about one path for all, rather it is about making the path work for everyone. title: Eric Insurance Limited (subject to Deed of Company Arrangement)
description: On 28 July 2025, Eric Insurance Limited, a small, privately-owned general insurer that provided add-on motor vehicle-related insurance products, announced that it had appointed McGrathNicol as Voluntary Administrator.
body: Background On 28 July 2025, Eric Insurance Limited, a small, privately-owned general insurer that provided add-on motor vehicle-related insurance products, announced that it had appointed McGrathNicol as Voluntary Administrator. On 16 September 2025 Eric and its creditors entered a Deed of Company Arrangement (“DOCA”). The DOCA came into effect from 19 September 2025 governs how Eric’s debts will be paid, including claims by its policyholders. Information for complainants Unfortunately, AFCA cannot consider any new or open cases against Eric Insurance as it would not be possible to resolve complaints efficiently, effectively or fairly, as required under AFCA Rule A.2 and section 1051(4)(b) of Australia’s Corporations Act. This is because any AFCA outcome cannot affect what Eric Insurance will have to pay its creditors. This means if you lodge a complaint with AFCA, it is appropriate we exercise our discretion to not consider the complaint. This is in line with our general discretion to exclude complaints under Rule C.2.1. Our decision to exclude complaints has not been made lightly. The types of complaint AFCA can consider are set out in our Rules. If you would like more information about our Rules, you can go here. Compensation Scheme of Last Resort AFCA has assessed that complaints against Eric Insurance are not eligible for the CSLR. In June 2023, the Australian Government passed a law which provided that the CSLR will be able to consider a claim for compensation if the consumer has received a favourable AFCA determination that the financial firm has not paid (for example, because it has gone into liquidation), and the financial service or product involved is one of those defined in the law. Under the law, the CSLR is limited to complaints about: In addition, on 31 January 2024, the Australian Government amended the conditions of the authorisation for AFCA to be Australia’s financial sector external dispute resolution scheme. This change means we cannot issue a decision (also known as a determination) in a complaint against a financial firm for certain financial services and credit activities if that firm: providing financial product advice that is personal advice to retail clients dealing in securities for retail clients engaging in a credit activity within the meaning of the National Consumer Credit Protection Act 2009 (Cth). would be unlikely to pay any compensation awarded; and was not authorised (because of its licensing conditions) to provide the products or services within scope of the CSLR. does not meet these licensing conditions, and AFCA is satisfied it would be unlikely to pay any compensation awarded. Administrators and DOCA Administrators On 28 July 2025, Eric Insurance entered voluntary administration. This means an external party is now managing the company. The appointed administrators are Shaun Fraser and Kathy Sozou of McGrathNicol, who have now also been appointed to administer the DOCA. If complainants and other creditors

title: Expanding Jurisdiction: Building on trust and capability
body: Annual Review 2024-25 Download the PDF Previous Annual Reviews Contents AFCA’s expanding jurisdictions reflect the confidence that Government, regulators, industry and the community have placed in AFCA’s ability to deliver fair, independent and effective dispute resolution. Over the past seven years, AFCA has demonstrated that we can manage complex disputes at scale, adapt to changing financial markets, and provide trusted outcomes for consumers and financial firms alike. From scams and digital assets to Buy Now Pay Later products and beyond, AFCA’s remit needs to change in step with the evolving financial landscape, to ensure fair outcomes for both consumers, and support for members, both existing and new. As we take on these new areas, we remain focused on ensuring Australians have the confidence that when harm occurs in financial services, there is a trusted place to turn to for complaint resolution and redress. We will continue to work closely with Government, regulators, industry and consumer groups to support the effective design and implementation of these new jurisdictions.
