title: Our people - Faye
body: “Our leaders sincerely want us to succeed, and they will do everything to help us get to where we want in our careers.” - Faye, Senior Dispute Resolution Specialist Faye's story I've always had a passion for fairness. In my family, I'm the fairness police. It's natural for me; I want things to be fair. Every day, I meet people who are at their most vulnerable, and I can help them regardless of whether AFCA finds in their favour. In my role, I can use my prior customer service and communication skills, and my experience and knowledge in superannuation and insurance to navigate and assess each complaint assigned to me. My background also helps me educate and enlighten complainants and challenge and question financial firms when needed. I love knowing I get to help the public daily! I recently had a complainant that didn't receive an outcome in their favour, and instead of being upset, she thanked me for taking the time to listen to her and investigate her complaint. I was overwhelmed by how this woman, possibly at the worst time of her life, was still very gracious and thankful towards me. I thanked her for keeping such an open mind and wished her all the best. I love that although we may not always find in favour of complainants, we can still impact their lives positively. My role at AFCA is very rewarding. Not all cases are resolved in the same way, and sometimes, you have to think outside of the box. Each day brings an opportunity and a sense of accomplishment to advance a complaint to the next stage or resolve it through small or significant actions. I am most proud of all the people I have touched since joining AFCA five years ago. I hope they remember me as someone who was fair, kind and sincerely cared for their wellbeing. I am proud of this because not everyone has the opportunity to help people in a very practical way. At AFCA, so many brilliant people have come from different industries and walks of life but with one passion - to give people their lives back. The culture is supportive, inclusive, and positive overall. Our leaders sincerely want us to succeed, and they will do everything to help us get to where we want in our careers. As a 47-year-old migrant woman, I don't feel overlooked here. People are acknowledged, every opinion is valued, and being from the Philippines is embraced with genuine interest. Everyone is appreciated, and I love the work-life balance—it's something I couldn't find anywhere else. It's like the cherry on top!

title: Our people - John
body: "It's important customers have an opportunity to be heard, receive support, and access justice." - John, Head of Membership Services (acting) John's story Being kind is my superpower. This comes from lived experiences, my family, and my upbringing. To be an effective leader, I focus on understanding people, their values, and what makes them tick, aligning this with our vision and purpose, and doing so with heart. Our work is more than just resolving consumer complaints and influencing industry best practices. It's about making a positive impact that reaches far and wide. The landscape is complex, but every small step we take, and every good deed we do brings us closer to our vision. And that's something we can all be proud of. In my role leading a business unit that supports our members, it’s important to empower our leaders and their people by giving clarity of purpose and linking their actions to our vision. It’s about creating opportunities for people to thrive in their careers and bring their authentic selves in the workplace. For example, I've chaired the Inappropriate Behaviour Working Group, which has delivered protocols on how our people can best support customers in difficult situations or with inappropriate behaviour. The processes and procedures ensure our team feels secure, delivers exceptional customer service, and addresses complaints in a safe environment. One of the most challenging aspects of my work is engaging with customers who don't know about our services, or that help is available. Promoting our services and positively and strategically influencing the industry so that all Australians can access and know that AFCA is there to help is so important. At AFCA, our leaders take the time to connect, understand, and support our team members, helping them to be their best. Their effective leadership and performance provide a safe and nurturing environment. This approach identifies emerging talent and ensures that everyone feels valued and appreciated. It's truly heartwarming to see how colleagues have grown from the start of their careers.

title: Our people - Maria
body: "I love working with diverse individuals and like-minded people who all believe in the integrity of the Ombudsman's Office." - Maria, Conciliator Maria's story I knew I always wanted to study law; as it provides many options. But I never wanted a corporate career because I believe in social justice. Growing up in a financially struggling, vulnerable family, I was always acutely aware of injustice; the inability to get a ‘fair go’ or have a voice that would be heard and listened to. AFCA is fair and just to all; regardless of standing or privilege, you will be heard here. We guarantee an equal and fair approach to all. ‘Leading with the heart’ ensures parties are respected, heard and assisted in providing information to help them move through the process in a supported, professional, and impartial way. At AFCA, we're changing people's lives in meaningful ways. For example; whether it’s scam funds being returned, guarantees removed or rebuilding a home, its helping people move on with their lives after devastating events by achieving just and fair outcomes. I am proud of this because of the actual impact on people's lives and livelihoods. The culture at AFCA is amazing! As a solo parent, hybrid working has changed my life, and it's incredible. I now have a wonderful work-life balance so that I’m 100% engaged at work and still feel as connected at home as when I’m in the office, and available to my children. AFCA has fun social events, too. We recently had a Case Management System – a pioneering new technology - implementation celebration in Sydney and Melbourne, morning tea and a Halloween-themed karaoke night. AFCA also has The Women's Network, which I'm part of, and other inclusion and belonging groups. Everyone can get involved. When I started at AFCA, I was a lawyer with no specialist training in investments or banking. I had mentors and managers at AFCA who supported me, took the time to meet with me and encouraged me. AFCA provides expert training and has incredible resources that allowed me to build on my drafting skills and become proficient and an expert in all areas at AFCA including superannuation, life and general insurance. I am always encouraged in this learning environment and AFCA supports me in maintaining my practicing certificate. I am now also a nationally accredited mediator. I had and still have leaders encouraging me to be the best I can be. Now that I've been at AFCA for over ten years, I feel great satisfaction in training and mentoring others and watching employees learn and grow as I did. I have also established rewarding and enduring friendships. I thrive in my role as a Conciliator. I have a unique job and feel privileged to have it because I'm working across this amazing ocean of like-minded people with the same vision as me, and that is standing up to resolve complaints for consumers and small businesses in a fair and just way. I am proud of the team approach in ensuring these outcomes.

title: Our people - Natalie
body: “Most organisations sell things. Our focus is fairness. How wonderful is that?” - Natalie, Lead Ombudsman - Banking & Finance Natalie's story Fairness: there is such privilege in being given a role that allows you to bring fairness to situations where it might have been lost. Our role isn't just to help everyday Australians who need support resolving a dispute that is heavily affecting their financial and mental wellbeing but it's also to help businesses improve their practices. I'm a lawyer by trade and have ‘done good’ in various roles, but this role is different. At AFCA, I help resolve disputes between consumers and financial firms. Ideally, issues are resolved directly, but if not, we objectively assess the situation, applying the law and fairness principles. This role significantly impacts people's lives and provides consumers with confidence. For a purpose-driven organisation to be successful, it must be led by purpose-driven executives. And we're lucky to have that. Watching the ripple effect that creates for my colleagues and me is quite inspiring. It also creates a safe space of authenticity, vulnerability, and leading with heart. It allows us to show our authentic selves in our work, and that's what I love about the job. At AFCA, we seek guidance and take inspiration from leaders putting themselves out there, which sometimes means challenging industry practices to get the right outcomes. Our leadership doesn't shy away from that; everybody takes great confidence and inspiration from that. It's not a role where you can expect to go home every day having made everybody happy. That said, AFCA does an excellent job of supporting people. Helping them understand that they will face emotions and criticism at times. Our work is very public. But we all stand together knowing that we do a good job and it's for the greater good. I'm proud of my career progression at AFCA, but I'm prouder of some of the outcomes we've delivered for people who are compromised. That may be in terms of access to help or for people in vulnerable situations such as domestic violence. We receive complaints from First Nations people, people living in regional or remote areas, and where their first language is not English. We can help people to access the complaints resolution process when they haven't been able to do it themselves.

title: Our people - Paul
body: "I love working with diverse individuals and like-minded people who all believe in the integrity of the Ombudsman's Office." - Paul, Operations Support Administrator Paul's story Before starting at AFCA, I was a technician on the road and also worked in the television industry. I was very active and on the move. Due to becoming legally blind, I was forced to change careers. I did a pilot program at Vision Australia, and through this, I went for an interview at AFCA and got the job! I was unsure how I would go doing something totally different and foreign, especially at the age of 60 when I started. AFCA has been incredibly supportive in transitioning, setting everything up to work properly for me. Their ongoing leadership and care have provided immense support and allowed me to be at my best. Over the last seven years, I have been fortunate to navigate across a variety of roles. First, starting in Customer Service, then with secondments in Case Support, Workflow and Finance departments, and now full-time in Operations Support. My current role encompasses administrative tasks, including sorting bulk emails to new complaints, assigning them to the correct case or actioning translation requests, sorting inbound and outbound physical mail and preparing ASIC requirements for lawyers. Every day, I do my best and do more than expected when I can, like helping team members and assisting them with their workload. My manager shows care and support for me and my wellbeing and acknowledges my efforts. As a team, we are a great support and help to each other. People are willing to pitch in if we see something that needs to be done. If we see someone is overwhelmed; we just jump in. It's a good culture, and everyone is committed to helping others. I recently suffered a repetitive injury in my left hand, and my manager and AFCA have been very supportive in helping me manage it and continue to do my work. My manager always reaches out and ensures I am taking my breaks and looking after myself. I enjoy the flexibility of hybrid working with three days at home and two days in the office. No matter where I am based, I am set up to do my job to the best of my ability. AFCA has an unsurpassed employee focus. There's no doubt that it's an excellent culture. There's great support, recognition and employee focus.

title: Our people - Raina
body: “AFCA is really great place to grow if you put yourself forward. You will get results and it’s a very positive space to put your energy into.” - Raina, Business Operations Analyst Raina's story My grandmother helped raise me; she's very empathetic and had a long career in sales and customer service. I learned a great deal from her, including how to really listen to people, take that knowledge in, and how to use it in a positive way. I use this skill daily in my role as a Business Operations Analyst. It's important to have good people-listening skills, empathy, and a strong desire to learn and do good. Listening to people, hearing their needs, and actioning next steps are pivotal. My journey at AFCA began as a Customer Service Officer. Through my hard work and skill set, I've progressed to my current role supporting the Customer Service team. I don't have a financial services sector background, but AFCA has provided me with good training facilitated by top-tier trainers with process guides and thorough resources available to use at any time. Leadership at AFCA really cares and if you put in the effort, there are so many pathways here. AFCA truly is a great place to work. That’s because of the people I work with. AFCA lives and breathes what it promotes, and we have amazing top-down support. I've had great ongoing support, learnt a lot of industry and situational knowledge from seniors and leadership, and been given the opportunity to help support my peers further by taking on the Business Operations Analyst role. This role really let me develop a strong support foundation for my team, and to me that is very important. I was a User Acceptance Tester for the new Case Management System for 6-8 months before launch, so I got very knowledgeable in my area and other areas of the business. In preparation for going live, I did a lot of team training, process guides, and knowledge sharing both in person and online. On launch, I was the go-to support person for any issues that popped up. I reported any bugs to the business, built reporting for leadership, and helped with general support for the team. I have also joined the AFCA Ally Network, which heavily supports LGBTQIA+ staff and includes training and awareness throughout the business; I am very proud to be part of this as a trans woman. My role allows me the flexibility to work from home three days and two days in the office per week. During the new system training and launch, I tended to come in every day even though it was not required, but it ensured that everyone felt supported and cared for and people were around if they needed assistance.

title: Our people - Sam
body: “Each stage of my journey at AFCA has been instrumental in shaping my expertise and broadening my professional horizons.” - Sam, Dispute Resolution Specialist Sam's story Where I am now is where I've always wanted to be. Since starting at AFCA, I wanted to be a General Insurance Case Manager. A friend working at AFCA shared a foundational role in the Customer Service department, which I applied for and got the job. Coming from an IT and Australian Defence Force background, I just wanted to get my foot in the door and learn about the business. After two and a half years, I started outgrowing my role, and my manager suggested I go into the Pathways Program. I completed three months of learning on the job in the Jurisdiction team, learning how to write letters, how general insurance works, product disclosure statements, and every facet of the role. It was the best thing they ever did for me; it's like a ‘try before you buy’. During the program, I was in my Rules role for six months when an opportunity came up in General Insurance. I applied and was successful in another promotion! I've also had the opportunity to be a Change Champion for the rollout of our new Case Management System. I am proud to play a key role in assisting others as they learn and adapt to our new system. It allows me to leverage my knowledge and enthusiasm for continuous improvement to help my colleagues navigate changes smoothly. Contributing to their success and fostering a supportive learning environment during this transition is rewarding. I identify as gay. In the past, when I started a new company, you never knew how open you could be. But, in the first two weeks, I learned that our CEO is part of the LGBTQIA+ community, and that made me feel comfortable from the outset. You can just be yourself. You don't have to hide. I'm the co-chair of our Ally Network, which helps to provide a better workplace for people to be themselves, share their stories, and celebrate days of significance throughout the year. We recently invited Australian Olympic diving champion, Matthew Mitchem, to talk to the whole company. I was so proud of that moment. AFCA's ‘Take 3 on Wednesday’ initiative has significantly boosted my wellbeing. It's been the best thing they've ever done. The initiative encourages taking a short break to relax and recharge and has given me a valuable opportunity to step back from my work, refresh my mind, and maintain a healthy work-life balance. It's a simple yet effective practice that helps me stay focused and energised throughout the week. AFCA truly values work-life balance, allowing me to excel professionally while maintaining my wellbeing. It’s been instrumental in my ability to perform at my best.

title: Our people - Tami
body: "My opinion and my voice matter at AFCA. My voice is part of something big and dedicated to making the world better." - Tami, Dispute Resolution Specialist Tami's story Focusing on my faith is very important to me. In 2023, I started to wear a hijab. A lot of family and friends advised me to be careful and prepare myself for negative sentiments and reactions. But I couldn't have been more wrong. After an all-unit meeting, my manager reached out and congratulated me. He acknowledged this was a big step and how important it was to me. I also received an email from David Locke commenting on how proud he was. I burst into tears as I did not expect this kindness. This is one of my most precious memories, and the reaction I received was beautiful. I feel like even though I am a little more covered, this is showing more of me than ever before. I am seen for who I authentically am at AFCA. Everyone's health and wellbeing are a priority at AFCA. By focusing on my health and wellbeing, I have found a confidence that I have never had in my life. I am performing at a capacity with skills I never even knew I had. I have found that this has changed my lifestyle for the better. This is my favourite thing at AFCA. I choose to work at AFCA because a big part of my life is devoted to helping others. I studied behavioural science and want to make a difference in the world. Before starting, I thought the emphasis on fairness would make me feel satisfied in my role but there is so much more! Being able to think for myself, in relation to case assessment and having Ombudsmen and senior management ask me for my views is empowering. I love the sense of ownership, respect for people's work and insights, and encouragement to be involved in projects and initiatives. The most challenging part of working at AFCA is what I love the most: the case management aspect of my job. But it's challenging in the best way because it teaches you how to look at things from a different perspective every time. There’s a real sense of accomplishment when you have written something fair in all circumstances. I love the investigational aspect of this role, and how what we do can give everyone their best chance to bring their case forward. I'm a middle child and always used ‘out of the ordinary’ explanations or justifications to escape situations. This creative investigative spirit is embedded in me to help others. I have always had an innate fear of public speaking. I can't do it, and it makes me nervous. However, AFCA has a conciliation part of the case management stage. After attending some conciliations and internal support and training, I became accredited in conciliations, and I can run them myself now. I'm so proud of this, it's confidence-building, and I have grown as a person.

title: Our process
description: Understand AFCA's process for handling complaints involving elected representatives. Discover how we ensure fairness and transparency in our decisions.
body: Resolving a financial complaint​ Complaint lodged with financial firm​ Tell your financial firm – many problems are fixed this way.​ Complaint not resolved​ Still not happy? Contact AFCA. ​ Complaint lodged with AFCA​ We will work directly with you and your financial firm to help you reach a fair agreement. Most complaints are resolved within 60 days.​ Registration and Referral​ We refer the complaint back to your financial firm and ask them to work with you. If you are unable to agree on an outcome, the complaint moves on to case management.​ Over half complaints resolve at this stage. Case Management​ An AFCA case worker investigates your complaint, including asking you to provide evidence that supports your claim. This can include notes, photographs, contracts and more.​ Decision​ If you and your financial firm cannot reach an agreement during case management, we will make a decision about what is fair, taking into account laws, codes of practice and all the relevant information provided.​ Related links The process we follow Make a complaint Financial hardship Referring people to AFCA​ If a constituent contacts you with an unresolved complaint about their financial firm. Then AFCA may be able to help.​ You can: direct the consumer to our website, or share our contact information such as email and phone, or lodge a complaint on their behalf. ​

title: Our Teams
body: Careers Home Why AFCA Our Teams Joining Us Careers News Be a part of our team and lead the way in fairness At AFCA, our teams are composed of dedicated, knowledgeable and empathetic individuals passionate about making a difference. Spanning functions across Dispute Resolution, Customer Service, Corporate and Employee Resource Groups, our people bring diverse skills and experience to ensure fair and independent resolution of financial complaints. Our team works collaboratively to foster trust, transparency and positive outcomes for all who seek our assistance. Explore open opportunities > Customer Service Our Customer Service Team manages the day-to-day interactions with our customers, ensuring the delivery of fair and independent dispute resolution for consumers and small businesses. They provide an efficient and high-quality service to all who seek assistance from AFCA. Learn more Dispute Resolution Our Dispute Resolution Team manages the resolution of disputes, ensuring the delivery of fair and independent services for consumers and small businesses. Learn more Jurisdiction and Decision Makers Our Jurisdiction and Decision Makers manage the determinations of complex financial disputes, ensuring the delivery of fair and independent resolutions. Learn more Corporate Services Our Corporate Services Team manages the operations of AFCA, ensuring the smooth and effective functioning of all departments. Learn more Careers news Discover our latest updates, insights and stories highlighting how AFCA employees are making a meaningful impact in their careers and shaping our industry.

title: Outcomes
description: Explore AFCA's MP Hub outcomes section for detailed information on dispute resolution results and the impact on financial firms and consumers.
body: We have a range of methods to resolve complaints. We select the method, or combination of methods, that we think is most likely to resolve the complaint fairly and efficiently. Resolving a financial complaint​ Complaint lodged with financial firm​ Tell your financial firm – many problems are fixed this way.​ Complaint not resolved​ Still not happy? Contact AFCA. ​ Complaint lodged with AFCA​ We will work directly with you and your financial firm to help you reach a fair agreement. Most complaints are resolved within 60 days.​ Registration and Referral​ We refer the complaint back to your financial firm and ask them to work with you. If you are unable to agree on an outcome, the complaint moves on to case management.​ Over half complaints resolve at this stage. Case Management​ An AFCA case worker investigates your complaint, including asking you to provide evidence that supports your claim. This can include notes, photographs, contracts and more.​ Decision​ If you and your financial firm cannot reach an agreement during case management, we will make a decision about what is fair, taking into account laws, codes of practice and all the relevant information provided.​ Types of remedies we can award​ We can require that a financial firm:​ See Outcomes AFCA provides for monetary limits. pay a sum of money for financial loss​ meet a claim under an insurance policy (general and life) ​ release security over a debt​ repay, waive or vary a fee or interest charged. Related links Outcomes AFCA provides The process we follow Make a complaint Financial hardship

title: Outcomes AFCA provides
description: Understand the possible outcomes AFCA can provide in resolving financial disputes, including compensation, refunds, and more. Read more here.
body: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) will generally try to first resolve a complaint by informal methods and reach a settlement between you and the financial firm through negotiation or conciliation. However, if we make a formal decision, there are a number of outcomes – also called ‘remedies’ – that we can provide under our Rules. Find out more about our complaint resolution process, including when we will make a decision. Section D of our Rules and Operational Guidelines provides greater detail about remedies, and when we can apply them. Remedies for general complaints We may decide that a financial firm or complainant must undertake a course of action to resolve a complaint. We will only provide a remedy if we decide that you have incurred loss or harm that was caused by the financial firm’s conduct. Some of the remedies we can award have financial value and others do not, and the examples provided on this page are not exhaustive. We may decide another outcome is more appropriate. We can’t impose punitive, exemplary or aggravated damages. When deciding on the remedy, we often seek to achieve, as nearly as possible, either: to place you in the position you would have been in if the conduct of the financial firm had not caused the loss; or to compensate you for your loss to the extent AFCA holds the financial firm responsible for the loss. Examples of outcomes we can provide Some examples of remedies include: the payment of a sum of money the forgiveness or variation of a debt the release of security for debt the repayment, waiver or variation of a fee or other amount paid to, or owing to, the financial firm or to its representative or agent including the variation in the applicable interest rate on a loan the reinstatement, variation, rectification or setting aside of a contract the meeting of a claim under an insurance policy by, for example, repairing, reinstating or replacing items of property in the case of a complaint involving a privacy issue with an individual – that the financial firm should not repeat conduct on the basis that it constitutes an interference with the privacy of an individual or that the financial firm should correct, add to or delete information pertaining to the complainant in relation to a default judgment, not enforcing the default judgment in relation to privacy-related complaints, to make an order that is generally consistent with the declarations available to the Information Commissioner when they make a decision under section 52 of the Privacy Act an apology. Remedies in cases of financial difficulty In a financial hardship complaint, the remedies we can provide include varying the credit contract. Examples of credit contract variations include: If we conclude that there has been a breach of the credit provider’s obligations to provide financial hardship assistance, we will also consider whether the complainant has suffered any financial or non-financial loss. title: Overview of complaints
body: Annual Review 2024-25 Download the PDF Previous Annual Reviews Contents AFCA received 100,745 complaints in 2024‑25. This is 4% less than in 2023‑24 which had a record high of 104,861 and is the second year in a row we have exceeded 100,000. Complainants secured $390,862,905 in compensation and refunds. While there were declines in some product areas, increases in others contributed to continued high volumes. Specifically: ¹ During 2024‑25, scam‑related complaints dropped a notable 45% to 5,977 which led to less banking and finance complaints. While this is encouraging, the impact of scams remains serious, and the numbers increased towards the end of the financial year. While Government and industry are making progress, much more work is needed to prevent scams and protect consumers. The most complained about financial products were: The top three complaint issues were: The collapse of several financial firms adversely drove up investments and advice complaints. Self‑managed superannuation fund (SMSF) complaints significantly increased by 95%, which comprised nearly a third of complaints in this area. Failure to act in the client’s best interest complaints rose a worrying 124%. These trends highlight persistent risks associated with conflicted advice models and inappropriate use of SMSFs. Superannuation complaints were down, with a 39% drop related to delays in claims handling. While this is an improvement, service quality remains a key concern. A significant increase in add‑on insurance product complaints saw general insurance complaints rise 17%. Other general insurance areas remained stable. The persistently high volume of insurance complaints highlights the need for further action by industry to prevent complaints and reduce the need for consumers to come to AFCA for resolution, particularly where claim delays are the issue. AFCA encourages financial firms to review their IDR functions to ensure they are correctly identifying complaints and effectively resolving them as early as possible. Comprehensive vehicle insurance remained the most complained about insurance product. Delays in processing claims were often linked to broader industry challenges, which included vehicle parts’ shortages and limited skilled repairers. These challenges are not new and we consider that more can be done by insurers to meet customers’ expectations – particularly through proactive, clear and timely communication. Complaints about general insurance premium increases rose. AFCA has a very limited role in relation to product pricing. AFCA cannot generally consider a complaint about the level of a fee, premium, charge, rebate or interest rate. We cannot consider a complaint merely because a complainant is dissatisfied that their premium has increased, or is unhappy about the amount of the increase. However, we consider that insurers can better explain premium changes, including the effects of external cost pressures on premiums. We encourage financial firms to clearl

title: Paano ako maghahain ng reklamo sa AFCA?
description: Languages-Filipino
body: Tungkol sa AFCA Ang Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) ay isang independiyenteng sistemang panlabas sa pagresolba ng alitan. Ang AFCA ay nagbibigay sa mga indibidwal at maliliit na negosyo ng libre, makatarungan at naaangkop na serbisyo sa paglutas ng mga reklamo tungkol sa mga pinansiyal na produkto at serbisyo. Maari naming harapin ang iba’t ibang uri ng problema, kabilang ang: mga pagkakamali sa mga transaksyon sa bangko at mga pagkalista sa kredito (credit listings) hirap sa pagbabayad ng mga utang, mga credit card at panandaliang pananalapi (short-term finance) kung saan nagbago ang iyong pinansiyal na kalagayan pagtanggi sa claim sa seguro (tulad sa sasakyan, tahanan at mga nilalaman, mga alagang hayop, paglalakbay, proteksyon sa kita at trauma) di-angkop na payo sa pamumuhunan ang desisyon ng tagapangasiwa tungkol sa pamamahagi ng benepisyo sa superannuation. Ang magagawa namin Kung hindi mo naresolba ang iyong reklamo nang direkta sa iyong pinansiyal na kompanya, maaari mong kontakin ang AFCA. Matutulungan naming lutasin ang iyong reklamo nang walang gastos at hirap ng pagpunta sa korte. Hindi na kailangan pang kumuha ng payo sa batas o iba pang payo kapag naghahain ng reklamo maliban kung gusto mo ito. Ano ang maaari kong gawin kung dumaranas ako ng kahirapan sa pananalapi? Maaaring malagay ang mga indibidwal at maliliit na negosyo sa mga sitwasyon kung saan hindi nila matugunan ang kanilang mga kahilingan sa pagbabayad sa ilalim ng isang pasilidad sa kredito (tulad ng isang pautang sa bahay, pautang sa negosyo o credit card). Tinatawag namin itong kahirapan sa pananalapi. Gabay sa pagharap sa kahirapan sa pananalapi Serbisyo ng interpreter Kung hindi Ingles ang iyong unang wika, maaari ka naming bigyan ng libreng serbisyo ng interpreter. Tumawag sa libreng serbisyo ng interpreter sa 131 450, o tumawag sa amin sa 1800 931 678 * at aayusin naming mabigyan ka ng isang interpreter. Makipag-ugnay sa amin Kausapin ang aming kawani sa 1800 931 678*. *9 ng umaga–5 ng hapon na oras sa Melbourne. Libre ang mga tawag sa numerong ito mula sa mga landline sa Australya. Ang mga tawag mula sa mga mobile phone ay maaaring singilin, paki-tsek sa iyong carrier. Karagdagang impormasyon Paano lulutasin ang reklamo Form ng reklamo - napi-print na bersyon Gabay sa mga kumperensya ng pakikipagkasundo Gabay sa pagharap sa kahirapan sa pananalapi Polyeto tungkol sa paraan ng paglutas ng reklamo Feedback tungkol sa aming serbisyo Pinahahalagahan namin ang inyong feedback. Ang mga papuri, mungkahi at reklamo tungkol sa aming serbisyo ay nakakatulong sa amin para magpakahusay. Nagsisikap kaming maghatid ng mahusay na serbisyo sa lahat ng aming mga kostumer, ngunit alam namin na kung minsan ay may mga bagay na namamali. Kung sakaling mangyari ito, ipagbigay-alam lamang sa amin. Feedback at mga reklamo tungkol sa serbisyo ng AFCA Iba pang mga detalye ng contact Australian Financial Complaints Authority GPO Box 3 Melbourne VIC 3001 Email: info@afca.org.

title: Paid representatives information
body: Consumer portal The AFCA consumer portal provides complainants and their representatives a modern and intuitive way to submit and track their complaint throughout the AFCA process. Learn more This page has information for representatives who are paid to assist consumers with making a complaint about a financial firm. Paid representatives looking for information about their AFCA membership can visit the AFCA Member Portal. Representatives and AFCA’s service AFCA helps individuals and small businesses to sort out disagreements with their financial firm. We understand many complainants experience stress and, in some cases, financial strain, so our service is free and easy to access, without the need for representation. However, complainants sometimes prefer to have a representative help with their complaint, particularly when they need additional support due to illness, financial strain, or a disability. A representative can be a family member, friend, lawyer, financial counsellor, or someone who offers to represent them for a fee or other type of financial remuneration – we call this last group paid representatives. About paid representatives A paid representative is any person or business that is paid to represent and assist a complainant, but does not include accountants, lawyers, financial counsellors or other representatives who have professional bodies to monitor their conduct. Paid representatives can include, but are not limited to: Debt management firms (DMF) – which are firms who assist complainants with issues about consumer credit contracts for a fee or who charge an amount that is payable in relation to a service. These firms are now regulated and must hold an Australian credit license and be members of AFCA (ASIC Info 254). Insurance claims handling firms – representatives who provide claims handling and settling services about insurance products. These firms must hold an Australian credit license and be members of AFCA (ASIC Info 253). Any other entity who charges a fee (or may otherwise receive financial remuneration) to assist a consumer to lodge a complaint at AFCA. Expectations of paid representatives AFCA expects paid representatives to conduct themselves at a higher standard than voluntary, unpaid representatives. Under the AFCA Engagement Charter, all representatives, including paid representatives, are expected to: AFCA also expects paid representatives to only make a complaint with AFCA after they have gone through the IDR process. If the paid representative is unable to resolve the issue with the financial firm directly, they can then make a complaint with AFCA. As paid representatives are not a requirement to access our services, the fee/s paid to the representative will not be awarded as part of any compensation provided in the outcome of a complaint. act in the best interests of the complainant and avoid conflicts of interest act fairly and professionally towards AFCA and financial firms avoid lodging complaints that are

title: Paid representatives: providing information to AFCA
body: This guide outlines what information paid representatives should provide to AFCA when lodging a complaint, including any specific information AFCA requires for different types of complaints. This guide is also useful for complainants who need to share AFCA’s requirements with their paid representative. This guide is not exhaustive and will be updated regularly. If you have a complaint about a topic that is not listed below, or you have questions about the information we require, please contact info@afca.org.au. Making a complaint Before making a complaint to AFCA, paid representatives should first complain to the financial firm through their Internal Dispute Resolution (IDR) process. You can search for the IDR contact information of a financial firm here. We expect paid representatives to only make a complaint with AFCA after they have gone through the IDR process. If the paid representative is unable to resolve the issue with the financial firm directly, they can then make a complaint with AFCA. AFCA strongly prefers paid representatives to use our online complaint form as their primary method of lodgement to support the efficient handling of complaints. Information required for all complaints Please provide: Along with the information required for all complaints, paid representatives should provide additional information by complaint type. The complainant’s name and contact details, including their address, telephone number and email address Specific details, all key issues and all alleged errors or breaches by the financial firm with supporting documentation. It is not acceptable to include only a generic template response alleging errors not specific to the complaint at hand A signed and dated AFCA Agent Authority form If the complainant is a company, a signed and dated resolution of directors’ form, signed by the directors appointing the Paid Representative as the company’s representative at AFCA Documentation to show a complaint has previously been raised with the financial firm and an explanation of why the complaint remains unresolved If received, the financial firm’s IDR or EDR response Details of the outcome the complainant is seeking which aligns with any AFCA approach. Information required by complaint type Contents Responsible lending Default listing/credit reporting Financial difficulty Motor vehicle / pleasure craft insurance Home building and contents insurance Consumer credit insurance Travel / ticket insurance Add-on insurance GAP Insurance Tyre and Rim Insurance Extended Warranty Responsible lending Please provide: If the credit facility refinanced an existing facility, please also provide: If the credit disputed was a business facility, please also provide: Back to top A completed AFCA Statement of Financial Position form outlining the complainant’s financial position at the time any disputed credit application was approved Supporting documentation to show the complainant’s financial position at the time that any disputed cred

title: Panels
description: panels
body: AFCA uses expert Panels to make Determinations about particularly complex complaints we receive. Panel members are appointed by the AFCA Board based on their objectivity, qualifications, experience and relevant personal qualities. Each panel that is formed to make a Determination will normally have three members – a Chair (an AFCA Ombudsman), an industry representative and a consumer representative. AFCA has a large pool of Panel members covering consumer and industry specialist experience to draw on to ensure that the correct expertise is applied to each complaint. title: Perth Hills bushfires
description: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) has activated its significant event response plan following the Perth Hills bushfires being declared a catastrophe from 5 February 2021 by the Insurance Council of Australia (ICA).
body: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) has activated its significant event response plan following the Perth Hills bushfires being declared a catastrophe from 5 February 2021 by the Insurance Council of Australia (ICA). The significant event response plan is activated for events that can potentially result in significant numbers of related complaints coming to AFCA. It provides for early communication with relevant stakeholders and a more streamlined, expedited process for the resolution of related complaints. Have you been affected? If you have been affected by the Perth Hills bushfires, we encourage you to contact your insurance company. For help with the claims process, or if you are unable to contact your insurance company, please call the Insurance Council of Australia disaster hotline on 1800 734 621. How we can help you AFCA offers free and accessible dispute resolution services to consumers and small businesses impacted by this event. If you have raised a complaint with your insurance company but you have been unable to resolve the matter, you can then come to us for assistance. Please note, we are only able to consider your complaint once you have raised the matter with your insurer. If you encounter difficulties relating to your insurance claims which you are unable to resolve directly with your insurer, you can register your complaint with us using our online complaint form or by calling 1800 931 678. More information about the process we follow to resolve complaints is available on our website. Financial hardship People affected by natural disasters can experience both short-term and long-term financial difficulties, ranging from lost credit cards and problems accessing cash through to being unable to make loan repayments. Banks and other financial services providers generally provide assistance to customers in cases of genuine hardship. If you encounter difficulties relating to a financial hardship application, you can make a complaint to us online, or call us on 1800 931 678. More information on natural disasters and financial hardship can be found in this fact sheet. Please make sure you notify us that you have been affected by the Perth Hills bushfires when you make your complaint. Insurance claims – cooling off period The General Insurance Code of Practice Catastrophes section provides for a cooling offer period for claims arising from a natural catastrophe or disaster. If your insurer is a member of the General Insurance Code of Practice (the Code) and it finalised your claim within one month of a natural catastrophe or disaster, you have a 12-month cooling off period to check whether your claim included everything that was lost or damaged. The cooling off period is available to you even though you may have signed a release when your claim was finalised. If you think the assessment of your loss was not complete or accurate, contact your insurer and ask it to review your claim. Some insurers also have guidelines whi

title: Policies and MoUs
description: Explore AFCA's policies that guide our operations, ensuring transparency, accountability, and adherence to industry standards in financial dispute resolution.
body: Policies Privacy policy Privacy matters to us and we know it matters to you. We take people’s privacy very seriously and it is essential to us that we look after and secure information that we collect. Whistleblower policy Whistleblowers perform an essential function in the community, ensuring that public officials are held to account and private individuals operate within the confines of the law. Protection of whistleblowers is therefore integral to fostering transparency, promoting integrity and detecting misconduct. AFCA Modern Slavery Statement AFCA, as a values-based organisation, considers it an important responsibility to ensure that we identify and address any incidents of modern slavery in an appropriate and timely manner. FY-23 FY-24 FY-25 Memorandums of understanding MoU between AFCA and APRA AFCA and the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) have signed a memorandum of understanding (MoU) setting out how they will continue to work together to support a fair and efficient financial system. MoU between AFCA and ASIC AFCA and the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) have signed a memorandum of understanding (MoU) setting how how they will they will continue to work together to support a fair and efficient financial services sector. The MoU documents how ASIC and AFCA already engage, and will continue to engage, including through information sharing and other forms of cooperation and coordination. MoU between AFCA and CSLR AFCA and Compensation Scheme of Last Resort Ltd (CSLR Ltd) have signed a memorandum of understanding (MoU) setting out how they will work together to support a fair and efficient financial system.

title: Portal continuous improvement
body: Continuous improvement Portal enhancements Troubleshooting Portal enhancements We complete regular system updates to resolve issues and make enhancements. Your feedback helps us prioritise improvements that are most important to you. We share all portal enhancements after every system update, via email and on our website. System update: March 2025 We improved usability of the ‘Documents’ section on a case by allowing you to filter and sort documents by date, name, category, and who the document was provided by. You can also click on the document name to open the document and change how many results to show on each page when viewing. System update: March 2025 To give members and consumers more time to complete activities without having to log in again, we extended session times from 90 minutes to 8 hours for members, and from 30 minutes to 4 hours for consumers. All users now receive a warning five minutes before the session times out. System update: July 2025 We delivered a new, interactive case reporting function within the member portal. Using Case Reporting, you can view a list of financial complaints from all complaint stages and apply filters to narrow down the results before exporting the data as a CSV file. System update: July 2025 Case fee invoices now include the financial firm reference number, where that information is available on the case. This will help you review your invoices more efficiently and consolidate invoice information with your own records. You can also now download your invoice as an Excel file. System update: August 2025 Members can now manage their Systemic Issues and Reportable Matters (SARM) cases in the portal. Just like regular complaints, you can see your active SARM cases and complete any required actions on the case. Communication with AFCA about SARM cases can now be completed within the portal. You can also make a request, export your case and reassign your case to another team member within the portal. Portal feedback Your feedback helps us prioritise improvements and directly shapes our roadmap for future enhancements. You can share your feedback with our Membership team at any time. Contact us Portal news We recently completed a system update to enhance the consumer and member experience of our service. We are pleased to confirm the following improvements. We recently completed a system update to enhance your experience using the AFCA member portal. We are pleased to confirm the following improvements.

title: Portal continuous improvement
body: Continuous improvement Portal enhancements Troubleshooting Our commitment to continuous improvement Thousands of member contacts use our portal every day to resolve complaints with their customers. At AFCA, we are committed to providing a service we can be proud of, and that includes our systems and technology. We want our portal to facilitate fair and effective external dispute resolution for our members and their customers. Through your feedback and engagement, we have identified the following areas of focus to deliver a better portal experience. These are themes that are on our radar for future enhancements to the portal. Portal feedback Your feedback helps us prioritise improvements and directly shapes our roadmap for future enhancements. title: Portal continuous improvement
body: Continuous improvement Portal enhancements Troubleshooting Troubleshooting We are aware of the following issues that may be impacting your member portal experience. See below for temporary workarounds and the actions we are taking to resolve these issues. We are aware of an issue where a user attempts to log in to the portal but receives the error message: ‘A user with the specified credentials cannot be found’. When attempting a password reset, users are able to go through the reset process when they may not have an account. If so, they will receive the above message after going through this process. Some users are unable to update their email address in the portal. If this happens, contact our Membership Services team at membership@afca.org.au or 1300 56 55 62. Please ensure you are on the member portal log in page, not the consumer portal. You can check the URL in your browser to confirm this. We are working to improve this experience. Members are not able to enter an alternate email address as referral contacts at this time. We are exploring other ways for you to provide an alternate email in the future. Case referrals will continue to be sent to the EDR Manager email address. When a case is closed, reopened then closed again, the second closure letter is saving with the same name as the first letter. This means the date it was created in the documents list stays the same, but the contents of the letter changes. Refer to the date the document was created as the first closure date, then the contents of the document to show the second closure date. On target to be fixed in 2026. There are intermittent issues with the change of financial firm request, which means the request can't be submitted even when mandatory fields have been completed. We are also working to improve the visibility of AFCA's responses and communications to old and new financial firms when a financial firm is changed. If you have an issue when submitting a request, try starting a new case request. Our team will manually notify you of any change if we accept a change of financial firm request. On target to be fixed in 2026. We are aware of an issue where deactivating a contact and then re‑adding them with the same role (for example, removing then re-adding a Case Worker) does not reactivate their contact record. This will show in the portal as successful, but they won't be able to access the member account. If you are adding a contact who you previously deactivated, contact AFCA via a Member Request in the portal, or by email at membership@afca.org.au. Need help? If you encounter an issue with the member portal, please email our Membership team or call 1300 56 55 62. title: Previous CIO members - are you required to have AFCA membership?
description: Previous CIO Members
body: This matter has now been referred to ASIC. For more information please contact ASIC directly. The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) is seeking contact from ex-CIO members who are yet to finalise their membership with us. We have published a list of these financial firms below. We believe these organisations may need to be members of AFCA as a condition of their financial services or credit licence. If a financial firm holding a current financial services or credit licence is still operating, and has not joined AFCA in accordance with their licence requirements, they are likely to be in breach of their legal obligations. AFCA has been liaising closely with ASIC about financial firms that have not taken out AFCA membership and ASIC will be taking appropriate action against these firms. AFCA is asking organisations who were previously members of the Credit and Investments Ombudsman (CIO) and have not taken out AFCA membership, to check if they are on the published list. They then need to immediately take out or finalise their AFCA membership application if they are required to be a member, or contact ASIC to cancel their licence if they are no longer operating. Media release: AFCA seeks proof-of-life for 231 financial firms (28 November 2018) Who are the organisations AFCA is seeking? There are two groups AFCA is seeking contact from: Group 1: Were previous members of the CIO and have not applied for membership with AFCA despite having a requirement to do so as a holder of an Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) or Australian Credit Licence (ACL). Group 2: Were previous members of the CIO and have not applied for membership with AFCA, but do not hold a current AFSL or ACL – AFCA is attempting to clarify their requirement to membership and if they do in fact have a requirement to be a member of an EDR scheme. What do the listed organisations need to do? For information regarding financial firms in breach, please contact ASIC. Check the list AFCA has published If you are required to become an AFCA member you need to either: immediately apply for AFCA membership. This can be done online at AFCA’s website at https://www.afca.org.au/members/apply-for-membership; or if you are no longer operating a financial services business please contact ASIC and arrange to cancel your licence. Additional background information Generally, financial firms or persons that hold: are required by law to be a member of AFCA.[1] Entities that are Privacy Act participants and are required to be a member of a recognised external dispute resolution scheme may also need to take out membership with AFCA. [1] Some limited exemptions from holding membership of AFCA apply, mainly for financial firms that do not provide services to retail investors. See Table 2 in ASIC Regulatory Guide 267, Oversight of the Australian Financial Complaints Authority. an Australian Financial Service Licence (AFSL), an Australian Credit Licence (ACL) or are an Authorised Credit Rep

title: Previous consultation information
body: AFCA Scheme Amendment Authorisation On 19 February 2019, the responsible Minister changed the authorisation conditions for AFCA Limited to operate the AFCA scheme. The new conditions require AFCA to deal with complaints about conduct by financial firms (who are current members of AFCA) dating back to 1 January 2008. This expanded jurisdiction will operate for a period of 12 months from 1 July 2019. Complaints that come within the expanded jurisdiction will be subject to the remaining provisions of the AFCA Rules. The AFCA Scheme Amendment Authorisation outlines the new conditions, and to comply with these conditions, AFCA will need to amend its Rules and Operational Guidelines. Consultation on Rules amendments Consultation on the Rules amendments closed on Friday 12 April 2019. Feedback was sought based on the following consultation documents. Submissions received during the consultation period are published below. The feedback received will be considered and appropriate changes made, before the proposed amendments to the Rules are provided to ASIC for review and approval, which may result in further changes. It is anticipated that the relevant amendments to the Rules will be released in June 2019. Consultation paper Draft amendments to the Rules Draft amendments to the Operational Guidelines Submissions Please note: If a submission was marked confidential it will not be published below. If a submission contained specific complaint details or personal contact details they have been redacted for privacy reasons. title: Previous EDR schemes
description: Previous EDR schemes
body: On 1 November 2018, The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) replaced the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), the Credit and Investments Ombudsman (CIO) and the Superannuation Complaints Tribunal (SCT). You can make a complaint to AFCA online here. If you are looking for previous EDR scheme publications, including reports and submissions, you can find them here. If you have a complaint with a predecessor scheme The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) replaces the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), the Credit and Investments Ombudsman (CIO) and the Superannuation Complaints Tribunal (SCT). If you currently have a complaint with FOS, CIO or the SCT your complaint will continue to be dealt with by that scheme. If your complaint has already been ‘dealt with’ by that scheme (including if it was resolved by agreement, or if an assessment or a decision on the merits of your complaint was made), AFCA is unlikely to be able to deal with it a second time. Refer to AFCA’s Rules and Operational Guidelines for more information on time limits and other information about what complaints AFCA can consider. If you are not sure whether you have a complaint that can be considered by AFCA, please contact us on 1800 931 678. If you currently have a complaint with FOS, CIO or SCT please refer to the information below. The FOS and CIO dispute resolution schemes are being administered by AFCA. Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) All FOS disputes that were received by FOS before 1 November 2018 and that are open on or after 1 November 2018 will be handled under the existing FOS Terms of Reference. Credit and Investments Ombudsman (CIO) All CIO disputes that were received by the CIO before 1 November 2018 and that are open on or after 1 November 2018 will be handled under the existing CIO Rules. Superannuation Complaints Tribunal (SCT) All SCT complaints that were received by the SCT before 1 November 2018 and are open on or after 1 November 2018 will be handled under SCT’s jurisdiction. Complaints withdrawn from the SCT cannot be relodged with AFCA.

title: Privacy notices
body: Jump to: Privacy notice for complaint handling Privacy notice for calls to AFCA's mainlines Privacy notice for AFCA’s recruitment process Privacy notice for membership services This page sets out privacy notices for specific activities conducted by the Australian Financial Complaints Authority Ltd: For general information about how AFCA handles personal information, please refer to our privacy policy. If you have any questions about AFCA’s privacy notices, please contact privacy@afca.org.au. Complaint handling Call recordings on AFCA’s mainline Recruitment Membership services Privacy notice for complaint handling AFCA collects personal information when investigating and determining a complaint. The type of personal information that AFCA may collect includes identity and contact information, financial information and sensitive information. AFCA requires this information to properly investigate a complaint about a financial firm. By making a complaint to AFCA, you consent to AFCA considering your complaint against a financial firm/s in accordance with AFCA’s Rules. You authorise AFCA to use and disclose your information, including relevant sensitive information: You also authorise your representative (or that representative’s organisation), other complaint parties, or the financial firm to exchange your information, including relevant sensitive information, to other parties for the purpose of dealing with the complaint, and as required or permitted by law. Details about how we handle your personal information are set out in our Privacy Policy available here. Our Privacy Policy includes information on how you can access or correct your personal information that AFCA holds. It also sets out how you can make a complaint about how AFCA has handled your personal information. You may withdraw your consent for us to handle your personal information by contacting us, however we will be unable to resolve your complaint and we will continue to keep information that has been collected. If known at that time, we will also inform you of any other potential impact that withdrawing your consent may have. Withdrawing your consent will not impact how we comply with our legal and regulatory obligations. If you have any questions about how AFCA handles privacy, you can speak to an AFCA staff member or contact AFCA Privacy by email to privacy@afca.org.au. by exchanging it with the financial firm/s, other parties or external organisations where reasonably necessary for the purpose of dealing with the complaint, or as required or permitted by law by exchanging it with other financial firms where there are multiple complaints against different financial firms to another financial firm or dispute resolution service where AFCA decides it is appropriate they handle your complaint in connection to an industry code compliance investigation or monitoring activity if reasonably necessary, for this complaint to resolve another complaint that may or may not be related to this com

title: Privacy policy summary
body: Privacy matters to us and we know it matters to you. We take people’s privacy very seriously and it is essential to us that we look after and secure information that we collect. Our primary purpose in collecting any personal information, as defined in the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) (Privacy Act) and referred to in section 1.3 and throughout the privacy policy, is to resolve financial complaints that are lodged with us. This summary sets out key points of our privacy policy. You should refer to the privacy policy if you would like more information than what is provided in this summary. This current policy came into effect in March 2026 and may change over time. Scope We provide consumers and small businesses with fair, free and independent dispute resolution for complaints about financial firms. We collect, hold, use and disclose your personal information to carry out our functions and activities as an external dispute resolution scheme. Our Rules and Operational Guidelines set out how we deal with complaints and confidentiality requirements. Collection of your personal information There are three ways that we collect information. You give it to us. Most of your personal information (including sensitive information[1]) is provided by you directly or by your authorised representative when you lodge a complaint with us. This can include your name, email, address, date of birth and/or phone number. You may also provide personal information when you use our web chat or our portal services. We will also usually collect personal information if you apply for a job at AFCA or if you subscribe to our mailing lists. Your financial firm gives it to us. To resolve your complaint, your financial firm may provide us with information. We get it from other sources. Sometimes we may need to collect personal information from other people and organisations when we are dealing with a complaint, assisting regulators or seeking to improve our services. For example, we may collect personal information from: other parties to a complaint publicly available sources, if the information relates to our investigation of a complaint regulators or government agencies, including ASIC and the ATO other third parties, such as employers and reference checking agencies, during our recruitment process, artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted tools that generated information about you, and third-party suppliers to enable us to operate our service. We may also collect information through our website, surveys and social networking services such as publicly available pages on Facebook, LinkedIn, Twitter and YouTube. For example, we may use this information to improve our website and receive feedback from the community. We use Campaign Monitor and Vimeo to manage our mailing lists, event registrations and livestreams of events. For example, we may collect personal information from: other parties to a complaint publicly available sources, if the information relates to our investigation of a compl

title: Public Report: Independent Assessor July-December 2019
body: Reporting period: July-December 2019 Independent Assessor: Melissa Dwyer Key messages I received 71 complaints during the six months to 31 December 2019, an increase of 60% compared to the previous six months. A total of 78 complaints were closed during the period, 33 of which were closed upon the issue of an assessment. The other 45 were closed because they were specifically excluded from my jurisdiction by the Independent Assessor’s Terms of Reference. Forty two percent of complaints assessed had one or more issues substantiated. There was an average of six issues in each complaint, but the one complaint that was substantiated in full only contained one issue. During the period I recommended: apologies be made to 14 complainants a total of $6,200 non-financial compensation be paid other action be taken in two complaints. apologies be made to 14 complainants a total of $6,200 non-financial compensation be paid other action be taken in two complaints. body: Reporting period: July-December 2020 Independent Assessor: Melissa Dwyer Key messages I received 91 complaints during the six months to 31 December 2020, one fewer compared to the previous six months. A total of 85 complaints were closed during the period, 25 of which were closed upon the issue of an assessment. The other 60 were closed because they were specifically excluded from my jurisdiction by the Independent Assessor’s Terms of Reference, were withdrawn by the complainant or the complainant failed to respond to correspondence. 56% (14) of complaints assessed had one or more issues substantiated. There was an average of six issues in each complaint, but the one complaint that was substantiated in full had only one issue. During the period I recommended: apologies be made to 14 complainants a total of $4,550 non-financial compensation be paid other action be taken in two complaints. title: Public Report: Independent Assessor July-December 2021
description: Explore the findings of the AFCA Independent Assessor's public report for July-December 2021. This report details the complaints received, the outcomes of assessments, and the actions recommended to improve service quality.
body: Reporting period: July-December 2021 Independent Assessor: Melissa Dwyer Key messages I received 103 complaints during the six months to 31 December 2021, seven fewer compared to the previous six months. A total of 97 complaints were closed during the period, 29 of which were closed upon the issue of an assessment. The other 68 were closed because they were specifically excluded from my jurisdiction by the Independent Assessor’s Terms of Reference or the complainant failed to respond to correspondence. 62% (18) of complaints assessed had one or more issue that had not already been appropriately dealt with by AFCA. During the period I recommended: apologies be made to 14 complainants a total of $5,850 non-financial compensation be paid one other action be taken. Some examples of complaints that were substantiated are: timeframes not met (delays in case progression, delays in responding to service complaints, not providing updates) failure to acknowledge correspondence until follow up actions were taken by the complainants failure to explain why process-related decisions were made (such as merging or reallocating a complaint). apologies be made to 14 complainants a total of $5,850 non-financial compensation be paid one other action be taken. timeframes not met (delays in case progression, delays in responding to service complaints, not providing updates) failure to acknowledge correspondence until follow up actions were taken by the complainants failure to explain why process-related decisions were made (such as merging or reallocating a complaint). title: Public Report: Independent Assessor July-December 2022
body: Reporting period: July-December 2022 Independent Assessor: Melissa Dwyer Key messages I received 133 complaints during the six months to 31 December 2022, 28 more compared to the previous six months. A total of 133 complaints were closed during the period, 37 of which were closed upon the issue of an assessment. The other 96 were closed because they were specifically excluded from my jurisdiction by the Independent Assessor’s Terms of Reference or the complainant failed to respond to correspondence. I substantiated 58 issues, of which 49 (84.5%) had not been appropriately dealt with by AFCA prior to being raised with me. During the period I recommended: apologies be made to 26 complainants a total of $13,700 non-financial compensation be paid five other actions be taken. Some examples of complaints that were substantiated are: delays in considering a complaint and not providing updates during the delay failure to adequately respond to correspondence failure to follow AFCA’s process, such as to enable a complainant to object to a jurisdictional decision. apologies be made to 26 complainants a total of $13,700 non-financial compensation be paid five other actions be taken. delays in considering a complaint and not providing updates during the delay failure to adequately respond to correspondence failure to follow AFCA’s process, such as to enable a complainant to object to a jurisdictional decision. title: Public Report: Independent Assessor July-December 2023
body: Reporting period: July-December 2023 Independent Assessor: Melissa Dwyer Key messages I received 126 complaints during the six months to 31 December 2023, 3 more compared to the previous six months. A total of 127 complaints were closed during the period, 31 of which were closed upon the issue of an assessment. The other 96 were closed because: they were specifically excluded from my jurisdiction by the Independent Assessor’s Terms of Reference the complainant failed to respond to correspondence, or the complainant withdrew the complaint. I substantiated 47 issues, of which 41 (87.2%) had not been appropriately dealt with by AFCA prior to being raised with me. Of the 41 issues, five related to delays in dealing with the Independent Assessor complaints and the remaining were mainly issues relating to inadequate responses to calls or correspondence (14), delays in AFCA’s financial firm complaint handling/ lack of updates (13) and poor-quality information provided (four). During the period I recommended: apologies be made to 16 complainants a total of $4,650 non-financial compensation be paid seven other actions be taken. Some examples of complaints that were substantiated are: In relation to Communication: An AFCA assessment incorrectly stated the complainant had failed to provide requested information when, in fact, AFCA had not requested the information. This error was exacerbated by AFCA’s Service Complaints Team considering but not substantiating the error. AFCA failed to respond to the complainant’s correspondence and, after advising the due date for responding to the determination was on hold pending AFCA’s response to her service complaint, AFCA held her to the original date to accept or reject the determination (despite the service complaint not having been addressed). In relation to Fairness and Impartiality: AFCA failed to advise the complainant it would not be relying upon information he provided (perhaps misunderstanding why the information had been provided) prior to issuing an expedited determination. After the determination, the complainant explained why he thought the information was relevant, but AFCA did not refer his submission to the decision maker. In relation to Efficiency and Effectiveness: Complaints relating to delays in the financial firm complaint process were substantiated. Delays occurred most frequently at the point of allocation to a caseworker or decision maker. One complainant experienced a two and a half month wait before their complaint was allocated to a caseworker, during which time, they received no status updates from AFCA. they were specifically excluded from my jurisdiction by the Independent Assessor’s Terms of Reference the complainant failed to respond to correspondence, or the complainant withdrew the complaint. apologies be made to 16 complainants a total of $4,650 non-financial compensation be paid seven other actions be taken.
