title: Public Report: Independent Assessor July-December 2024
body: Reporting period: July-December 2024 Independent Assessor: Melissa Dwyer Key messages I received 143 complaints during the six months to 31 December 2024, 19 fewer compared to the previous six months. A total of 132 complaints were closed during the period, 30 of which were closed upon the issue of an assessment. The other 102 were closed because: they were specifically excluded from my jurisdiction by the Independent Assessor’s Terms of Reference the complainant failed to respond to correspondence, or the complainant withdrew the complaint. I substantiated 46 out of 146 issues (31.5%). During the period I recommended: apologies be made to 17 complainants a total of $8,050 non-financial compensation be paid two other actions be taken. Regarding substantiated issues: In relation to Communication: Communication issues continue to be a significant challenge. Of note, my office saw an increasing number of complainants with open AFCA cases seeking assistance, many of whom complain they failed to receive a response from AFCA staff despite multiple attempts to make contact. In two complaints, the caseworkers failed to advise they were taking extended leave despite requesting further information and being aware of the complainants’ personal circumstances. In another complaint, AFCA called the complainant multiple times despite their requesting on multiple occasions only to be contacted in writing, and took over a month to acknowledge the complainant’s email requesting a service complaint be opened. There were also a few complaints where I substantiated communications that were confusing and/or provided incorrect advice. In relation to Fairness and Impartiality: AFCA failed to escalate the complainant’s objection to the initial jurisdictional assessment to a senior staff member for their review and decision on whether to exclude the complaint before it closed the complaint. This was a procedural fairness failing. While the complainant provided general consent to AFCA sharing her submissions with the other parties to the complaint in her initial complaint lodgement, the complainant also made explicit statements to AFCA (verbally and in writing) indicating that she did not consent to AFCA exchanging particular documents. Despite that, AFCA failed to contact her prior to exchanging the information and shared unredacted personal information with the parties without her knowledge or consent. In relation to Efficiency and Effectiveness: Like 2023-24, many complaints about unreasonable delays in progressing and finalising financial firm complaints were substantiated during this period. Again, delays occurred most frequently at the point of allocation to a caseworker or decision maker. One complainant experienced a four-and-a-half-month delay before the complaint was allocated to a caseworker, during which time the complainant received no proactive update and followed up on the complaint’s progress four times. they were specifically excluded from my jurisdiction

title: Public Report: Independent Assessor July-December 2025
body: Reporting period: July-December 2025 Independent Assessor: Melissa Dwyer Key messages I received 245 complaints during the six months to 31 December 2025, 46 more than during the previous six months (199). A total of 227 complaints were closed during the period, 43 of which were closed upon the issue of an assessment. The other 184 were closed because: they were excluded from my jurisdiction under the Independent Assessor’s Terms of Reference the complainant failed to respond to correspondence, or the complainant withdrew the complaint. I substantiated 43 out of the 149 issues I considered (29%). During the period I recommended: apologies be made to 21 complainants a total of $6,550 non-financial compensation be paid two other actions be taken. Regarding issues substantiated by my office: In relation to Communication: The communication issues raised primarily related to inadequate responses to calls and correspondence and provision of poor-quality information. This included lack of updates on the progress of a complaint, providing unclear information, and inconsistent explanations about AFCA’s process or jurisdiction. In some matters, the quality of communication contributed to misunderstandings about what issues AFCA could consider, how a complaint would progress, or why certain decisions were made. In the six months, I have substantiated 18 out of 52 complaints relating to communication. In one complaint, AFCA incorrectly streamed the complaint to the superannuation team, despite the complaint relating to an investment account. As a result, the complainant received incorrect advice about AFCA’s ability to award non-financial loss compensation. In another complaint, AFCA failed to clearly inform the complainant that certain issues he raised were deemed outside AFCA’s jurisdiction until late in the process. In relation to Fairness and Impartiality: Fairness and impartiality continue to be the most common issues raised with my office. Complainants frequently alleged breaches of AFCA’s Rules, Operational Guidelines or other policies and procedures, as well as bias, failure to consider relevant information in decisions and a lack of procedural fairness. In the six months, I have substantiated 2 out of 35 complaints relating to fairness and impartiality. In one complaint, AFCA failed to acknowledge or consider certain issues raised by the complainant in their online submission. These issues were not included in the initial letter sent by the caseworker or AFCA’s preliminary assessment. In relation to Efficiency and Effectiveness: Delays in progressing and finalising complaints continued to be a concern this period. As in previous years, the most significant delays arose during the allocation and case management stages, with some complainants experiencing extended periods without contact or updates. I substantiated 21 out of 52 complaints relating to efficiency and effectiveness. In one matter, there was a three‑month delay in completing a jurisdictio

title: Publication order form
body: Publication and quantity Your details Are you also looking for a speaker? If you are ordering materials for an upcoming event, contact us at info@afca.org.au if you would like an AFCA representative to participate or present. Do financial firms need to keep AFCA brochures on hand? Our members are not required to keep AFCA brochures on hand, although they do need to ensure their customers are aware that they can bring a complaint to AFCA. Instructions on how to do this are provided at afca.org.au/members/telling-your-customers-about-afca/.

title: Purpose
description: Learn about the purpose of AFCA's Engagement Charter, aimed at fostering fair and effective resolution of financial complaints. Click here to read more.
body: AFCA’s Engagement Charter outlines AFCA’s expectations about how people using our service will engage with us and each other during our complaint resolution process. It also describes the service standards others can expect from AFCA. It is a living document, based on core principles and designed to respond to an ever- changing financial landscape. We may amend and expand on the principles set out in this document from time to time. AFCA’s role AFCA’s purpose is to provide fair, independent and effective solutions for individuals and small businesses who have a complaint about a financial product or service. AFCA has a specific and important role assisting individuals and small businesses to resolve complaints about financial services and products. We are: AFCA’s vision is to be a world class ombudsman service, by: impartial and independent – we do not advocate for either party or their position. fair and focussed on dispute resolution outcomes – if everyone cannot come to an agreement, it is our role to decide an appropriate outcome. improving practices and minimising complaints meeting diverse community needs; and being trusted by all. AFCA’s jurisdiction Our Rules set out the complaints we can consider, the procedures we use to resolve complaints and the remedies we provide. Our Operational Guidelines and Transitional Superannuation Guidelines set out how we interpret and apply our Rules. We can consider a broad range of financial complaints, including: errors in banking transactions and credit listings difficulty repaying loans, credit cards and short-term finance denial of an insurance claim (such as car, home and contents, pets, travel, income protection and trauma) investment and financial advice a trustee’s decision in relation to the administration of a superannuation account including distribution of a death benefit.

title: Raise a concern with AFCA - 1
body: Raise a concern with support We’re here to help if something hasn’t gone as expected with a financial service. If you’ve already tried to resolve it with your financial firm and it’s still not resolved, you can come to us. We’ll work with you to understand what’s happened and help find a fair outcome. You can get in touch online, by phone or by email, whichever works best for you. You can also ask for support at any time. We offer interpreter services (including Auslan) and access via the National Relay Service. Checklist Thank you for taking the time to answer our questions. It looks like you’re ready to make a complaint to AFCA. Click the button below to go to our complaint section and learn about the information you’ll need to make a complaint. Before you get started Before coming to us, it can help to contact your financial firm and ask them to review the issue through their internal process. You may also find it useful to: If the issue isn’t resolved, or you’re not satisfied with the outcome, you can come to AFCA for help. Keep notes of who you spoke to, key dates and any reference numbers Keep copies of any communication or documents What to prepare To help us understand your situation, it’s useful to have: You don’t need to have everything ready to get started, we can let you know if we need more information later. A short summary of what happened and the outcome you’re seeking Key dates, amounts and any reference numbers Any documents that support your situation (such as emails, letters, statements or screenshots) Get started What happens next 1) We review your details We check that your concern relates to a financial firm that is an AFCA member and that it’s something we can look into. 2) We share the information with the firm We provide the details to the financial firm so they can review the situation and try to resolve it with you directly. 3) We work towards an outcome If it isn’t resolved, we may try to resolve it informally, or move through a more detailed review and provide an outcome. You can choose to stop the process at any time. If court or enforcement action is underway, please let us know as soon as possible. Advocacy and support Accessibility and communication support We can support you to communicate with us in a way that works for you: Free interpreters are available (including Auslan). You can ask us or call 131 450 and request AFCA You can use the National Relay Service (TTY/voice 133 677, Speak & Listen 1300 555 727, or internet relay at nrscall.gov.au) If online isn’t suitable, you can also get in touch by phone or email. Independent advocacy and community support You may also choose to access independent support services, including: These services are separate from AFCA and can help you understand your options or act on your behalf. National Debt Helpline (free financial counselling) Small Business Debt Helpline Community Legal Centres and Financial Counselling Australia Aboriginal Legal Service and Women’s Legal Ser

title: Raise a concern with AFCA - 2
body: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) independently assists consumers and small businesses to make and resolve complaints about financial firms. When you complain to us, we follow a complaint resolution process that provides free and fair outcomes. However, you should be aware that there are certain complaints we can’t consider. Ways to get in touch Choose the option that suits you best. Our service is free and independent. Online Raise your concern online and track progress in one place. Call us Prefer to talk it through? We can help you get started and guide you through the next steps. Email Send us your details and any documents. We’ll guide you from there. Post (mail) You can write to us with what happened and the outcome you’re seeking. Accessibility and language support We can support you to communicate with us in a way that works for you. Interpreters, Auslan and accessible materials are available on request. What to know before you get started Start by contacting your financial firm. For many people, this is the quickest way to resolve the issue. If the issue isn’t resolved, you can come to AFCA and we’ll help you take the next step. You can raise a concern yourself, or ask someone you trust to act on your behalf. We’ll ask you to tell us: You can also share any documents that support your situation, such as emails, bills or letters. If you need extra help at any stage, just let us know. We can consider concerns about financial firms and the products and services they provide. In Australia, licensed financial services providers are required to be members of AFCA and take part in our process. You can come to us about a range of products and services, including: If you’re unsure whether your situation is something we can consider, you can still get in touch. We can review your information and let you know what options are available. If your situation falls outside what we can consider, we’ll help point you to other places that may be able to assist. You can come to us if you believe a financial firm has, for example: Some matters may fall outside what we can consider. This can include: Time limits also apply, and there are specific rules for some types of matters, such as superannuation. You can find more detail in our Rules, or we can help explain what applies to your situation. what happened what outcome you’re seeking credit products such as credit cards, store or gift cards and interest-free deals loans, including home loans, personal loans, investment loans and business loans banking deposits and payments, including online transactions, ATMs and mistaken payments insurance policies, such as home, travel, vehicle, business and life insurance superannuation investments, including managed funds, securities and derivatives financial advice made an error or not met their obligations not clearly explained fees, charges or interest rates provided misleading information about a product or service not responded appropriately to f

title: Raise a concern with AFCA - 3
body: Select your language You can view support options in your preferred language, including interpreter and accessibility support. Watch how the process works, check whether we can help, and choose the complaint channel that suits you best. Our service is free, fair and independent for consumers and small businesses. Video: A short introduction to AFCA, what we do, and what happens next. Choose how to complain You can lodge your complaint in the way that works best for you. Each channel gives you access to the same AFCA service. Online portal Lodge and track your complaint online. Call us Get help lodging by phone. Email Send us your details. Post (mail) Send your complaint by post. Need support in another language or format? We can arrange interpreters, Auslan and accessible materials. What to have ready Your name and contact details The name of the financial firm A short explanation of what happened What outcome you are seeking Any helpful supporting documents or correspondence What happens next We review your complaint and check whether it is something we can consider. We contact the financial firm and look at the information from both sides. We work towards a fair outcome as quickly as possible. Some complaints may progress to a formal decision if they are not resolved earlier. What can I complain about? This short video explains what happens after you contact us and how we work with you and the financial firm to resolve your complaint. Support and accessibility We want our service to be accessible. If you need support, there are different ways we can help. Support options Interpreters and translation support Accessibility assistance and alternative formats Auslan support Someone can act on your behalf if needed Complaint types and limits We can often help with Credit, finance and loan complaints Insurance Banking deposits and payments complaints Superannuation Investments and financial advice Financial hardship issues We may not be able to help if A court has already decided the matter The complaint is outside AFCA’s Rules The complaint is outside the relevant time limits The issue is not about a financial firm we cover What happens when I make a complaint? A quick checklist can make it easier to lodge your complaint and help us understand your issue sooner. If we cannot help If your complaint is outside our remit, we will let you know and point you towards other support or information where possible. That could include community legal support, financial counselling, or another organisation better placed to help with your issue.

title: Raise a concern with AFCA - 4
body: You can raise a concern with us in the way that works best for you. AFCA is free and independent. You can choose how you get in touch and move through the process at your own pace. Check if we can help You can search to see if the financial firm is an AFCA member. This helps you understand whether we’re able to consider your situation before you get started. Getting ready to raise a concern Here are a few things that can help you prepare: You can still come to us if the issue isn’t resolved or you’re not satisfied with the response. Contact your financial firm first, they may be able to resolve the issue quickly Think about what happened and the outcome you’re seeking Decide if someone will act on your behalf (for example, an authorised representative) Gather any documents that support your situation If you’re in financial difficulty, you may wish to prepare a Statement of Financial Position. What to expect We’ll work with you and the financial firm to find a fair outcome as efficiently as possible. This may involve resolving things early, or moving through to a formal decision by an Ombudsman if needed. How it works Tell us your story and share any relevant documents We work with you and the financial firm to try to resolve the issue If it isn’t resolved, we may provide a preliminary view or make a decision. Ways to lodge a complaint Online portal Set up an account in minutes. You can submit your information, upload documents and track progress all in one place. Call us Prefer to talk it through? We can help you get started and explain what happens next. Email Send us your details and any documents. We’ll guide you through the next steps. Post (mail) You can write to us with what happened, who was involved, and the outcome you’re seeking. Forms and supporting documents Forms and authorities Statement of Financial Position If you’re experiencing financial difficulty, this form helps you share details about your income, expenses, assets and debts. Agent Authority Use this form to authorise someone to act on your behalf. Other forms Access complaint forms, feedback forms and other downloadable documents. Accessibility and language support We can support you to communicate with us in a way that works for you: You can also: Interpreters are available, ask when you call 1800 931 678 You can use the National Relay Service (NRS) and provide our number 1800 931 678. Information in other languages is available on our website. Contact us View information in other languages Not sure where to start? If you’re unsure whether we can help or which option to use, we can guide you through your options and help you get started.

title: Rappsville and Long Gully Road Fires significant event response
description: */ /*-->*/ The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) has activated its significant event response plan following the Rappsville and Long Gully Road NSW Fires, being declared a catastrophe on 11 October 2019 by the Insurance Council of Australia (ICA). The significant event response plan is activated for events that can potentially result in significant numbers of related complaints coming to AFCA.
body: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) has activated its significant event response plan following the Rappsville and Long Gully Road NSW Fires, being declared a catastrophe on 11 October 2019 by the Insurance Council of Australia (ICA). The significant event response plan is activated for events that can potentially result in significant numbers of related complaints coming to AFCA. It provides for early communication with relevant stakeholders and a more streamlined, expedited process for the resolution of related complaints. Have you been affected? If you have been affected by the Rappsville and Long Gully Road Fires, we encourage you to contact your insurance company. For help with the claims process, or if you are unable to contact your insurance company, please call the Insurance Council of Australia disaster hotline on 1800 734 621. How we can help you AFCA offers free and accessible dispute resolution services to consumers and small businesses impacted by this event. If you have raised a complaint with your insurance company but you have been unable to resolve the matter, you can then come to us for assistance. Please note, we are only able to consider your complaint once you have raised the matter with your insurer. If you encounter difficulties relating to your insurance claims which you are unable to resolve directly with your insurer, you can register your complaint with us using our online complaint form or by calling 1800 931 678. More information about the process we follow to resolve complaints is available on our website. Financial hardship People affected by natural disasters can experience both short-term and long-term financial difficulties, ranging from lost credit cards and problems accessing cash through to being unable to make loan repayments. Banks and other financial services providers generally provide assistance to customers in cases of genuine hardship. If you encounter difficulties relating to a financial hardship application, you can make a complaint to us online, or call us on 1800 931 678. More information on natural disasters and financial hardship can be found in this fact sheet. Please make sure you notify us that you have been affected by the Rappsville and Long Gully Road bushfires when you make your complaint. Insurance claims – cooling off period The General Insurance Code of Practice Catastrophes section provides for a cooling offer period for claims arising from a natural catastrophe or disaster. If your insurer is a member of the General Insurance Code of Practice (the Code) and it finalised your claim within one month of a natural catastrophe or disaster, you have a 12-month cooling off period to check whether your claim included everything that was lost or damaged. The cooling off period is available to you even though you may have signed a release when your claim was finalised. If you think the assessment of your loss was not complete or accurate, contact your insurer and ask it to revie

title: Receiving Banks and Unauthorised Opening of Accounts
body: On 12 March 2026 AFCA's jurisdiction expanded to include investigating complaints involving receiving banks and unauthorised opening of accounts by scammers in a complainant’s name Consumers and small businesses can lodge complaints against a receiving bank where scam funds were received. They can also lodge a complaint against any bank where an unauthorised account was opened in their name without their consent or authority. In February 2025, the Australian Parliament legislated a new Scams Prevention Framework. This framework is yet to start. Further information is available on the Scams Prevention Framework page. Further information on the law and codes is available in ‘Scams overview’, ‘Authorised transactions’ and ‘Unauthorised transactions’. AFCA’s jurisdiction has expanded Scams are becoming increasingly sophisticated and widespread, prompting a shift in government policy toward a more holistic approach to consumer protection and redress. Previously, AFCA could only consider scam-related complaints that are lodged against the customer’s own bank and involve transactions made from the customer’s own account. This can leave consumers without recourse when scams involve multiple banks. From 12 March 2026, AFCA can investigate scam-related complaints about: The scam transaction or unauthorised account opening in the complaint does not need to have occurred on or after 12 March, but it must meet the eligibility criteria under AFCA’s Rules and must be lodged within AFCA's standard time limits. Under the standard time limits, the complaint must be lodged before the earlier of: These jurisdictional changes apply to all banks promote accountability and protections for consumers. AFCA's expanded jurisdiction is an important step towards the whole-of-ecosystem approach to tackling scams that will come into place through the Scams Prevention Framework. For more information read the Factsheet - Receiving Banks and Unauthorised Opening of Accounts. What you can do now for scam-related complaints the opening of a bank account or credit facility in a consumer’s name without their consent or authority, such as where the complainant’s identification documents have been used to open an account or to obtain credit. the receipt, internal movement or transfer of the consumer’s funds by a bank in connection with a scam, including where the consumer is not a customer of that bank. within six years of becoming aware (or should reasonably have become aware) they suffered the loss; and within 2 years of an Internal Dispute Resolution (IDR) Response from the bank (if received prior to submitting an AFCA complaint). If you are currently impacted by a scam, you should first raise any complaints directly with the bank involved. This gives the bank an opportunity to resolve the issue through its internal dispute resolution process. If you are unhappy with the handling of, or response to, your complaint, you can access AFCA’s free and impartial external dispute resolution

title: Receiving Banks and Unauthorised Opening of Accounts
body: Background The Federal Government issued a new authorisation condition in March 2025 requiring AFCA to expand its jurisdiction. Under the expanded jurisdiction from 12 March 2026AFCA can accept and investigate all banks involved in the flow of scam funds, not just the victim’s bank. This change recognises that scams often involve multiple financial institutions. It also allows AFCA to consider complaints from consumers who may have had unauthorised accounts opened or credit taken out in their name without their consent or authority. Banks play a critical role in preventing and responding to scam-related complaints. AFCA's expanded jurisdiction is an important interim step towards the whole-of-ecosystem approach to tackling scams that will come into place through the Scams Prevention Framework. AFCA is committed to supporting its bank members through this transition. We will share resources to help banks understand and navigate the complaint process. We’ve also developed FAQs below. AFCA’s expanded jurisdiction Prior to March 12, 2026, AFCA was generally limited to investigating complaints where the financial firm has a customer relationship with the complainant. AFCA’s jurisdiction has expanded to introduce Rule B.2.1(j), which allows us to consider scam-related complaints about a bank where there is no customer relationship, if the complaint relates to either: the opening of a bank account or credit facility in the complainant’s name without their consent or authority. the receipt, internal movement or transfer of the complainant’s funds by a bank in connection with a scam. We’re here to help AFCA expects receiving banks to complete their IDR process before the complaint reaches us. To support members to adapt to these changes AFCA has published FAQs and Member's Forum Recording.

title: Referral resolution rate and non-response rate
body: When assessing performance resolving complaints at the refer-back stage, referral resolution rate and non-response rate are useful measures to show how well a firm is communicating with complainants and engaging with AFCA’s process. Referral resolution rate Referral resolution rate reporting looks at the result of all referrals that were made by AFCA back to the financial firm. It measures the proportion that were closed at referral rather than progressing to case management. It is intended to measure a firm’s effectiveness at resolving complaints early, during AFCA’s registration and referral process. What does the referral resolution rate show? A high referral resolution rate shows that a firm is effective at resolving complaints early. This is supported by having skilled and empowered internal dispute resolution teams, with appropriate expertise and communicating with complainants early. Opportunities may exist for a financial firm with a high referral resolution rate to explore ways to resolve complaints directly with their complainants without the need for the complaint to be referred to AFCA. Reviewing touchpoints within a firm prior to the complaint being raised with AFCA may identify opportunities to prevent the escalation of future complaints to AFCA. In comparison, a low referral resolution rate shows that firms are unable to resolve complaints at registration, with complaints progressing to AFCA’s case management stage. This may be because the complaint is complex and will be unable to be resolved at the referral stage. It may also reflect that a firm needs to further invest in its complaint resolution function to ensure it is sufficiently resourced with the right balance of human skills, subject matter expertise, time management skills and communication skills. How referral resolution rate is calculated The “referral resolution rate” is calculated using the following formula: RRR (%) = Total closed in referral statuses / (Complaints closed in referral statuses + Complaints progressed to case management) X 100 Non-response rate The non-response rate measures the percentage of complaints that a financial firm did not respond to during the initial refer-back stage of the AFCA process. The refer-back stage is the period when AFCA refers a complaint back to a financial firm. What does the non-response rate show? The non-response rate is a simple way to see how effective a financial firm is at communicating with complainants and AFCA and working to resolve complaints. A low non-response rate shows that the firm is communicating during IDR, while a higher rate indicates a firm is not providing a consistent IDR service. A high non-response rate reflects that the firm is not responding to complaints within expected timeframes. This may be because the firm is not suitably resourced to meet its internal dispute resolution obligations. title: Reflect Reconciliation Action Plan
description: Learn about AFCA's commitment to reconciliation, including initiatives and actions taken to promote understanding and collaboration with Indigenous communities.
body: Acknowledgement of Country AFCA acknowledges the Traditional Custodians of this land and we pay our respects to Elders past, present and future – for they hold the songlines, the stories, the traditions, the culture and the hopes of First Nations Australia. This land is, was, and always will be traditional First Nations Country. We also acknowledge and pay our respect to the Traditional Custodians of the lands on which we work, including the Wurundjeri, Boonwurrung, Wathaurrung, Daungwurrung and Dja Dja Wrung peoples of the Kulin nation and the Gadigal people of the Eora Nation. As a national ombudsman scheme that provides services across the country, AFCA wants to be an organisation that delivers its service to First Nations peoples in a culturally informed, respectful and accessible way. We must purposefully consider and work to transform how AFCA can deliver services for the benefit of our First Nations customers. In order to do this, we have developed a Reflect Reconciliation Action Plan (RAP). Read the AFCA Reflect Reconciliation Action Plan “We are at the beginning of our reconciliation journey and our Reflect Reconciliation Action Plan is the first step." David Locke, Chief Ombudsman and CEO AFCA’s Reflect RAP outlines the actions we will take over the next 12 months to work towards our vision for reconciliation. It demonstrates the commitment all our people have towards creating an environment that supports greater economic and financial participation and inclusion. The actions we have committed to in our RAP will focus on strengthening the relationships, respect and opportunities for First Nations peoples, by building trust and understanding. We also want to ensure a deeper understanding throughout our organisation of First Nations peoples and cultures. “The Reflect RAP serves as a guide to building meaningful, genuine and mutually beneficial relationships with First Nations peoples, communities and businesses across all areas of our organisation, so we can deliver culturally sensitive and accessible services. In this way we will contribute to reconciliation through financial and economic inclusion.” Dr June Smith Deputy Chief Ombudsman and Chair of AFCA’s RAP working group Read the AFCA Reflect Reconciliation Action Plan About the artwork In recognition of our Reflect RAP, AFCA commissioned artwork by Edwin Lee Mulligan for our Melbourne and Sydney offices. The artwork is symbolic for our people and is a constant reminder of our reconciliation journey. Edwin Lee Mulligan named the artwork ‘Ngalimba’, meaning loving energy: finding strength to help others. At AFCA, we walk alongside people from different backgrounds, to ensure fair outcomes to financial disputes. Edwin Lee Mulligan says: “This painting is about the first time I saw my Grandfather’s Country. Walking this Country, I realised each step represented each passing day. This work is about finding your inner strength to walk with others." About the artist Edwin is a Walmajarri an

title: Regulatory Guide 271
description: Learn about AFCA's alignment with ASIC's Regulatory Guide 271, detailing updated IDR procedures and complaint handling timeframes effective from October 2021.
body: On 30 July 2020, ASIC released Regulatory Guide 271 (RG 271), which updated requirements for how financial firms should deal with complaints under their Internal Dispute Resolution (IDR) procedures. RG 271 came into effect on 5 October 2021 and replaced the existing Regulatory Guide 165. AFCA will consider a financial firm’s obligations under RG 271 on all complaints received by a financial firm on or after 5 October 2021. For more information read RG 271 and a summary of the updates on the ASIC website. Changes to AFCA’s processes While RG 271 focuses on expectations for financial firm’s IDR, AFCA has updated some processes to align with RG 271’s new timelines and requirements. New maximum IDR timeframes Some complaints lodged with AFCA which have not been through the financial firm’s internal dispute process are referred back to the firm with updated timeframes. Learn more Extension of IDR and post-IDR refer back time at AFCA RG 271 allows financial firms to exceed the maximum IDR timeframe in certain circumstances. AFCA has updated its IDR and post-IDR extension request policy, having regard to RG 271. Learn more ASIC Regulatory Guide 271 webinar On Wednesday 15 September, AFCA held a special webinar event about ASIC Regulatory Guide 271. The webinar was facilitated by AFCA Head of Operational Delivery Tim Goss, with presentations from ASIC Senior Executive Leader of Behavioural Research and Policy Sarah Edmondson and AFCA Executive General Manager of Operational Delivery Diana Ennis. Watch the webinar recording here Download the presentation slides here If you have any questions about AFCA’s processes please email info@afca.org.au

title: Responsible Lending Approach Consultation summary
description: Discover AFCA's stance on responsible lending practices, aimed at protecting consumers and ensuring fair lending standards in the financial industry.
body: From 31 July to 11 September 2023, AFCA invited members, consumer groups, industry bodies and other stakeholders to submit feedback and questions during consultation on its draft Approach to Responsible Lending. This page provides a summary of the consultation, the feedback and questions raised and AFCA’s response. About Responsible Lending Responsible Lending complaints are about consumer credit provided for personal, domestic or household purposes, such as personal loans, credit cards, and home loans. The Responsible Lending Approach explains how we consider these complaints, including: how we assess a financial firm’s compliance with responsible lending obligations how we determine a fair outcome where a firm breaches its responsible lending obligations, and how we calculate loss and assess benefits to determine compensation. Updates to the Responsible Lending Approach – May 2025 In February 2025, AFCA commenced a check-in consultation with financial firms and consumer groups about how the Responsible Lending Approach is operating in practice. We received written and verbal feedback which generally covered five key themes. You can read a summary of the themes and overview of key changes below. You can read the updated Approach here. Key feedback themes We acknowledge this feedback and have been doing significant work across our teams to embed the Approach and provide additional support to our people. We will continue this work. We have made some changes to the wording of the Approach to clarify some of these queries and clean up some language. We have reviewed the existing wording of the Approach in light of the feedback and in some cases we believe the existing wording is sufficient. For example, on page 41 we outline when and how we will apply interest to compensation amounts. On pages 43-44, we discuss how we determine the appropriate interest to apply to adjusted debts for secured loans where a complainant retains an asset. We have considered whether deposits should continue to generally form part of complainants’ gross loss, and we are satisfied they should, as outlined in the tables in Guide One. We have made some changes to the wording of the Approach to clarify some of these queries, and provided further guidance about how lenders may resolve inconsistency between benchmarks, declared expense amounts and verification material . We have also considered the guidance about interest rate buffers on page 25, and we have made a small clarification that APRA guidance applies to residential mortgage loans, not just residential home loans. We are comfortable the remainder of that section accurately reflects our approach to interest rate buffers on new and existing variable rate loans. Stakeholders expressed a desire for more issues to be covered in the Approach, including unjust transactions, pre-NCCP Act loans and broker conduct. We understand why consumer groups and financial firms would like to see us expand the scope of the Approach, but we

title: Royal Commission updates
body: The Royal Commission into Misconduct in the Banking, Superannuation and Financial Services Industry delivered its final report on 1 February. AFCA welcomed the recommendations made by Commissioner Hayne as well as the Government’s acceptance of its recommendations. AFCA stands firmly on the side of fairness and we believe these recommendations are critical to ensuring that customers and small businesses are treated fairly. Unpaid Determinations The Australian government has setup a fund to pay consumers and small businesses who had determinations from the Financial Ombudsman Service(FOS) and Credit and Investments Ombudsman (CIO) but never received payment. If you were awarded a determination by FOS or CIO that was not paid,you may be eligible to receive payment under this fund Find out more information on unpaid determinations. Media releases related to the Royal Commission: AFCA welcomes ASIC approval for legacy complaints 18 June 2019 Financial firms required by law to co-operate with AFCA 6 April 2019 Consultation now open on AFCA Rules changes 18 March 2019 AFCA to accept legacy financial complaints from 1 July 2019 20 February 2019 AFCA's statement on the Royal Commission's Final Report 4 February 2019

title: Rules amendment consultation September 2020
description: Review the proposed amendments to AFCA's rules from September 2020, focusing on improving the dispute resolution process for financial complaints.
body: The Superannuation Complaints Tribunal will cease operations after 31 December 2020. AFCA needs to amend its Rules to ensure there are appropriate arrangements in place to address the following possible events: The proposed changes will allow AFCA to consider these complaints and will not otherwise affect what complaints it can consider. any remaining complaints currently with the SCT are unable to be finalised prior to the SCT ceasing operations, or any matters that are before the Federal Court on appeal from the SCT are not finalised prior to SCT ceasing operations and require remittal back to be determined again or finalised in accordance with the Court’s directions. Consultation on Rules amendments Consultation on the Rules amendments closed on Friday 16 October 2019. Feedback was sought based on the following consultation documents. AFCA will be reviewing all submissions made and will release further information regarding the Rules changes in due course. It is anticipated that the relevant amendments to the Rules will be released by January 2021. Consultation paper Draft changes to the Operational Guidelines Submissions Please note: If a submission was marked confidential it will not be published below. If a submission contained specific complaint details or personal contact details they have been redacted for privacy reasons. title: Rules and guidelines
description: Rules and guidelines
body: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) is governed by a set of Rules. These Rules are approved by ASIC, in accordance with the requirements of the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) and are explained in more detail in our Operational Guidelines. AFCA's Rules and Guidelines are published to provide complete transparency into how we operate. Since coming into effect on 1 November 2018, there have been several important updates to the Rules and Guidelines which you can read below. Rules Approved Rules released 12 March 2026 Approved Rules released 1 July 2024 Approved Rules released 7 March 2024 This new version of the Rules includes: Approved Rules released 2 January 2024 Approved Rules released 13 January 2021 Approved Rules effective 25 April 2020 Approved Rules effective 21 February 2020 Approved Rules effective 1 October 2019 Approved Rules effective 30 June 2019 Approved Rules effective 1 November 2018 This version of the Rules included several changes including an amendment to AFCA’s jurisdiction to include the ability to investigate scam-related complaints involving receiving banks and unauthorised accounts opened by scammers in the complainant’s name. This followed a change to AFCA’s Authorisation Conditions by the Federal Government which introduced this change. Read more. This version of the Rules includes several significant changes, which are outlined here. The new Section H ensures AFCA’s Rules are consistent with the AFCA Scheme Amendment (2024 Measures No. 1) Authorisation 2024, issued by the responsible Minister on 31 January 2024, effective 1 February 2024 complies with ASIC’s legislative instrument and direction issued on 29 February 2024 requiring AFCA to make this Rules amendment to comply with the AFCA Scheme Amendment (2024 Measures No. 1) Authorisation 2024 applies to complaints made but not decided before 1 February 2024 and to complaints made after 1 February 2024 where specific conditions are satisfied. ensures AFCA’s Rules are consistent with the AFCA Scheme Amendment (2024 Measures No. 1) Authorisation 2024, issued by the responsible Minister on 31 January 2024, effective 1 February 2024 complies with ASIC’s legislative instrument and direction issued on 29 February 2024 requiring AFCA to make this Rules amendment to comply with the AFCA Scheme Amendment (2024 Measures No. 1) Authorisation 2024 applies to complaints made but not decided before 1 February 2024 and to complaints made after 1 February 2024 where specific conditions are satisfied. title: Rules changes to consider Legacy complaints dating back to 1 January 2008
description: Understand the rule changes allowing AFCA to consider legacy complaints dating back to January 1, 2008, providing a pathway for historical dispute resolution.
body: AFCA Scheme Amendment Authorisation On 19 February 2019, the responsible Minister changed the authorisation conditions for AFCA Limited to operate the AFCA scheme. The new conditions require AFCA to deal with complaints about conduct by financial firms (who are current members of AFCA) dating back to 1 January 2008. This expanded jurisdiction will operate for a period of 12 months from 1 July 2019. Complaints that come within the expanded jurisdiction will be subject to the remaining provisions of the AFCA Rules. The AFCA Scheme Amendment Authorisation outlines the new conditions, and to comply with these conditions, AFCA will need to amend its Rules and Operational Guidelines. Consultation on Rules amendments Consultation on the Rules amendments closed on Friday 12 April 2019. Feedback was sought based on the following consultation documents. Submissions received during the consultation period are published below. The feedback received will be considered and appropriate changes made, before the proposed amendments to the Rules are provided to ASIC for review and approval, which may result in further changes. It is anticipated that the relevant amendments to the Rules will be released in June 2019. Consultation paper Draft amendments to the Rules Draft amendments to the Operational Guidelines Submissions Please note: If a submission was marked confidential it will not be published below. If a submission contained specific complaint details or personal contact details they have been redacted for privacy reasons. title: Rules changes to identify financial firms in published determinations
description: Learn about the rule changes enabling AFCA to identify financial firms in published determinations, enhancing transparency in the complaint resolution process.
body: Rules amendment consultation May 2019 AFCA is committed to being open, transparent and accountable to the public. As part of our new transparency measures, AFCA has proposed to identify financial firms in our published decisions. We have drafted changes to Rule A.14.5 that will enable us to identify financial firms in published determinations. This will apply to determinations issued after the changes take effect, subject to the changes being approved by ASIC. Consultation on Rules amendments Consultation on the Rules amendments closed on Thursday 20 June 2019. Feedback was sought based on the following consultation documents. Submissions received during the consultation period are published below. In response to feedback and suggestions raised in stakeholder submissions that we received during the public consultation on the proposed change, the Operational Guidelines have been revised to clarify the circumstances in which AFCA will not publish a determination. Unless there are compelling reasons not to publish, the decision will be published. Consultation paper Draft changes to the Rule A.14.5 Draft changes to the Operational Guidelines ASIC Approval On 21 August 2019, ASIC approved the proposed Rules change and the associated change to the Operational Guidelines. This followed the AFCA Board considering all submissions and feedback provided during the public consultation, and taking into account our commitment to providing open, transparent and accountable services to the public and enhancing consumer confidence in financial services. The changes take effect on 1 October 2019 and will apply to Determinations issued on or after that date for complaints lodged with AFCA on or after 1 November 2018. Submissions Please note: If a submission was marked confidential it will not be published below. If a submission contained specific complaint details or personal contact details they have been redacted for privacy reasons. title: Rules consultation FAQ
body: Responses to queries raised during the consultation We thank stakeholders for their engagement in the consultation process. Below we have answered some of the questions that came up during the consultation. Read the questions Quick links What is the Rules Review project? Why did AFCA propose amendments to its Rules? What is changing? What was the consultation process? How can I make a submission? I have a question about the Rules Review project Where can I find more information about the proposed amendments to AFCA’s Rules and Operational Guidelines? Will AFCA propose changes in the future? Background AFCA consulted on changes to its Rules and Operational Guidelines from 27 March – 22 May 2023. The consultation is now closed. The proposed changes were developed to address recommendations made in Treasury's Independent Review of AFCA – with some additional changes to help ensure our Rules and Operational Guidelines remain accurate, up-to-date and provide clearer guidance about AFCA’s jurisdiction and processes. ​ All of the proposed amendments respond to feedback from our stakeholders, including government, AFCA members, community stakeholders and AFCA colleagues. Frequently Asked Questions What is the Rules Review project? When AFCA welcomed the Independent Review in 2021, we identified recommendations that would require changes to our Rules and Operational Guidelines. In 2022, we commenced a program of work to review our Rules and Operational Guidelines and inform a set of draft changes that we could take to stakeholders. This process included engaging with an external independent expert to assist with drafting the changes. Engaging an expert during this process helped us to ensure the proposed changes are worded clearly and unambiguous. Why did AFCA propose amendments to its Rules? Treasury's Independent Review of AFCA made a number of recommendations that require Rule and Operational Guideline (OG) changes. To help us deliver on these recommendations, and following a comprehensive review and internal stakeholder feedback, we proposed a package of Rule and OG changes and consulted with stakeholders. In addition to responding to the Independent Review recommendations, the proposed amendments included some additional changes to help ensure AFCA’s Rules and OGs remain accurate, up-to-date and provide clear guidance about AFCA’s jurisdiction and processes. What is changing? AFCA proposed changes to its Rules and Operational Guidelines in the following general areas below. You can find a full summary of the proposed changes on our website: All of the proposed amendments respond to feedback from members, our AFCA colleagues, government and community stakeholders. The proposed changes will help us to provide a world-class ombudsman service – improving practices, minimising disputes and meeting diverse community needs. The management of unreasonable and inappropriate conduct within the scheme, to strengthen AFCA’s ability to deliver procedural fairness

title: Rules consultation submission
body: Privacy and publishing submissions Formal submissions will be published on AFCA's website after the consultation closes. AFCA may also choose to publish submissions made using this online form. This may include publishing your name and/or organisation. AFCA will not publish your contact information such as email addresses. Please note, we will not treat your submission as confidential unless you specifically request that we treat the whole or part of it as confidential. You will need to opt-out when completing the form below. Please refer to our privacy policy for more information about how we handle personal information. Remaining anonymous To complete the online form, you will need to provide a name and email address. If you would like to remain anonymous, you can instead make a submission via email or in writing. However, if you do remain anonymous, we will not be able to contact you to discuss your submission should we need to.

title: SA, Victoria and Tasmania severe storms October 2021
description: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) has activated its significant event response plan following the South Australia severe storms being declared a catastrophe by the Insurance Council of Australia (ICA) on 29 October 2021 and the Victoria and Tasmania severe storms being declared a catastrophe on 1 November 2021.
body: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) has activated its significant event response plan following the South Australia severe storms being declared a catastrophe by the Insurance Council of Australia (ICA) on 29 October 2021 and the Victoria and Tasmania severe storms being declared a catastrophe on 1 November 2021. The significant event response plan is activated for events that can potentially result in significant numbers of related complaints coming to AFCA. It provides for early communication with relevant stakeholders and a more streamlined, expedited process for the resolution of related complaints. Have you been affected? If you have been affected by the South Australia, Victoria and Tasmania October 2021 severe storms, we encourage you to contact your insurance company. For help with the claims process, or if you are unable to contact your insurance company, please call the Insurance Council of Australia disaster hotline on 1800 734 621. How we can help you AFCA offers free and accessible dispute resolution services to consumers and small businesses impacted by this event. If you have raised a complaint with your insurance company but you have been unable to resolve the matter, you can then come to us for assistance. Please note, we are only able to consider your complaint once you have raised the matter with your insurer. If you encounter difficulties relating to your insurance claims which you are unable to resolve directly with your insurer, you can register your complaint with us using our online complaint form or by calling 1800 931 678. More information about the process we follow to resolve complaints is available on our website. Financial hardship People affected by natural disasters can experience both short-term and long-term financial difficulties, ranging from lost credit cards and problems accessing cash through to being unable to make loan repayments. Banks and other financial services providers generally provide assistance to customers in cases of genuine hardship. If you encounter difficulties relating to a financial hardship application, you can make a complaint to us online, or call us on 1800 931 678. More information on natural disasters and financial hardship can be found in this factsheet. Please make sure you notify us that you have been affected by the South Australia, Victoria and Tasmania October 2021 severe storms when you make your complaint. Insurance claims – cooling off period The General Insurance Code of Practice Catastrophes section provides for a cooling offer period for claims arising from a natural catastrophe or disaster. If your insurer is a member of the General Insurance Code of Practice (the Code) and it finalised your claim within one month of a natural catastrophe or disaster, you have a 12-month cooling off period to check whether your claim included everything that was lost or damaged. The cooling off period is available to you even though you may have signed a release when your

title: Scam alert: how to check if a message is really from AFCA
body: Things to look out for and how to protect your information Be wary of emails, phone calls and text messages claiming to be from AFCA and don’t engage with an interaction you don’t believe to be genuine. AFCA will never: If you become suspicious of an interaction and are unsure how to proceed, you can: For those who don’t use our portals, we may ask for personal information over email related to the complaint. If you’re unsure why we require this information, contact your case owner otherwise you can contact our customer service team on the numbers above. Never share your personal passwords. Ask you to pay us for our services or request payment related to the external dispute resolution complaint process Send you a text message asking you to provide personal information Provide links within text messages Contact you about your complaint on social media Send an email to a member firm contact with a link to a file shared through Dropbox. We restrict our use of file-sharing services to Digital Pigeon and AFCA will send a separate email with details on how to access that file if relevant. Send a complainant an invoice. Be wary of fake invoices sent by scammers impersonating AFCA. Ask the person calling to identify your case number to verify they are genuine Phone us on 1800 931 678 (consumers) or 1300 56 55 62 (members) Check that the email ends in @afca.org.au or is from afca@feedback-immoment.com.au (these are legitimate AFCA contact details) You can also report a scam to ScamWatch.

title: Scams EDR Member Onboarding FAQs
body: Scams Prevention Framework (SPF) The Scams Prevention Framework (SPF) Act 2025 is a new law introduced by the Australian Government to strengthen scam prevention, detection, disruption and response obligations across regulated sectors. It forms part of amendments to the Competition and Consumer Act. The SPF establishes: Under the SPF reforms, AFCA will be the independent dispute resolution service people can turn to when their scam-related complaint has not been resolved with the organisations involved. Almost all SPF complaints usually involve several organisations like Authorised Deposit-Taking Institutions (ADIs) including banks, telecommunications providers or digital platforms (such as internet search, social media, and instant messaging). AFCA will start receiving complaints under the framework from 31 March 2027. AFCA’s role includes: The initial regulated sectors expected to be covered under the SPF include: If your organisation operates a Regulated Service within a Designated Sector (such as an ADI, telecommunications provider or digital platform) you must comply with the SPF obligations applicable to that service, including any relevant sector-specific codes. Where multiple Regulated Services are operated across different Designated Sectors, the applicable obligations and codes must be applied to each service accordingly. Additional sectors may also be brought into the framework over time through future legislation and sector codes made by the Minister. To confirm whether a specific entity falls within a regulated sector under the SPF, organisations should refer to the SPF Sector Designation Instrument, in addition to guidance published by ACCC (as the SPF general regulator) and Treasury. AFCA can only consider complaints relating to SPF scams if the relevant matters occur on, or after 31 March 2027. Scam prevention obligations. Sector specific rules and codes. Internal dispute resolution requirements (IDR). External dispute resolution pathways (EDR). Regulatory oversight and enforcement mechanisms. Reviewing complaints that could not be resolved internally by the regulated entities. Assessing whether regulated entities took reasonable steps to prevent, detect, disrupt, and respond to the scam, against their sector-specific SPF obligations. Providing independent dispute resolution. Facilitating outcomes, including remediation or compensation where appropriate. Reporting serious or systemic issues to regulators. Authorised Deposit-Taking Institutions (ADIs) including banks. Telecommunications providers. Digital platforms (such as internet search, social media, and instant messaging). What members need to know Your organisation is an SPF-regulated entity if it provides or conducts a Regulated Service within a Designated Sector. To confirm whether a specific entity falls within a regulated sector under the SPF, organisations should refer to the SPF Sector Designation Instrument, in addition to guidance published by ACCC (as the SPF general

title: Scams Prevention Framework
body: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority has been authorised as the single external dispute resolution scheme for scam complaints under the Scams Prevention Framework, effective from 1 July 2026. Organisations designated under the Framework are required to be AFCA members from 1 September 2026. Membership applications open 1 July 2026, and early application is encouraged to support onboarding ahead of this requirement. For newly designated sectors, information on how to become an AFCA member can be found here. What is the Scams Prevention Framework? The Scams Prevention Framework Bill is intended to strengthen protections against scams imposing obligations on in-scope sectors to prevent, detect, report, disrupt, and respond to scams. A key component of the bill is the establishment of a dedicated External Dispute Resolution scheme for scam-related complaints. The Government has expressed its intention to authorise AFCA as the External Dispute Resolution scheme for the first three sectors (banks, telcos, and social media companies). The Bill sets the overarching framework and imposes broad obligations on businesses to protect consumers from being scammed, with tough consequences for non-compliance. The Bill will be supported by: The Bill will also introduce a mandatory intelligence-sharing system to ensure timely reporting and collaboration across industry and government. You can read more about the new Framework here. Scams Prevention Framework Rules – to clarify obligations, liability framework and IDR obligations. Mandatory Sector Codes – that will introduce additional or more specific requirements for regulated entities in particular sectors. A multi-regulator framework, with the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) as the general regulator, with the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) and the Australian Communications and Media Authority (ACMA) playing their role in the relevant designated sectors. What does this mean for AFCA? This will make AFCA the single External Dispute Resolution scheme for scams complaints, covering telecommunications and digital platforms providers, in addition to financial firms. Once in effect, AFCA will be able to handle a broader range of scam complaints. This will see new member firms join AFCA, including those currently with a different External Dispute Resolution scheme and those unfamiliar with EDR or mandatory Internal Dispute Resolution (IDR) processes. AFCA is very experienced in handling scam complaints in the financial services sector and an expert in dispute resolution generally. In 2023-24, AFCA resolved 10,400 complaints related to scams in an average of 57 days. AFCA will actively work with government, regulators, consumer groups and the industries involved to help deliver this important reform. What will happen next now the legislation has passed? Although the legislation has passed, there is a lot of work to do before AFCA can accept complaints under the new

title: Scams Prevention Framework External Dispute Resolution: applications open for membership 1 July 2026
body: AFCA has been authorised as the single External Dispute Resolution (EDR) service for scam complaints under the Scams Prevention Framework (SPF). If your organisation is designated under the SPF, you must be an AFCA member from 1 September 2026. Organisations in the telecommunications and digital platforms sectors may now be required to join the Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) to handle scam-related complaints. This is a statutory obligation set out by the Australian Government in the SPF designation instruments, which require regulated entities to be members of the authorised external dispute resolution scheme. Applications open from 1 July 2026 Apply now Important deadline: Apply by 14 August 2026 to ensure your organisation is onboarded in time to meet the requirement to be an AFCA member from 1 September 2026. If you are required to become an AFCA member from 1 September, you must submit your completed application by Friday 14 August. Applications received after this date may not be processed in time, which could delay your membership start date. Welcome Do I need to do anything? If your organisation experiences difficulties with the application process or accessing the member portal, please email our membership team at membership@afca.org.au or phone 1800 931 678 (free call) or 1300 56 55 62 (Members). Banking sector (Authorised Deposit Institutions and Australian Financial Services Licence holders): If you are already an AFCA member, no action is required. If you are not a member, and your organisation is providing a regulated service in a designated sector under the SPF, you will be required to become a member from 1 September 2026. Telecommunications providers and digital platforms: You may now be required to become an AFCA member, in addition to other memberships you may have. AFCA is the sole EDR scheme authorised to deal with scam complaints under the SPF. Not sure? First, check the official designation instruments. If you’re still unsure, seek legal advice. How to apply Apply for AFCA membership in three steps: There are many benefits to becoming an AFCA member. Visit our members page to learn more. Confirm you are a regulated entity. If unsure, check with your regulator before applying. Submit your application online and pay your registration fee. Apply through the AFCA Member Portal – find out more here. Apply before 14 August 2026. High demand is expected – we strongly recommend early applications. What does AFCA membership cost? For the 2026-27 financial year, the annual registration fee for AFCA membership is $415.57 AUD (including GST). SPF EDR funding The Australian Government has provided AFCA with a grant to support the establishment of the Scams EDR program of work, including preparation and readiness activities. Additional funding is required to ensure the scheme is fully operational from commencement, with the capability, systems and governance needed to deliver timely and fair outcomes for consumers and me

title: Scams: things to look out for and how to protect your information
description: Learn about common financial scams and how to protect your information, with tips and advice from AFCA to safeguard against fraud. Read more about how to protect yourself.
body: Scammers use dishonest or illegal activities like phishing, fraudulent behaviour and threats to steal personal or financial information. How to check if a message is really from AFCA AFCA sees a wide variety of complaints involving scams including investment, romance, invoice hacking, telecommunication, and Australian Government agency scams (such as those purporting to involve the police or the tax office). If you share or store personal information like credit card details or bank account passcodes online, you could be at risk of a scam. This page outlines what scammers can do with your personal information, how you can avoid a scam, and what to do if you think you are a victim. For more information, you can visit Scamwatch or the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC). Why scammers want your personal information Your personal and financial information is valuable. Scammers can use this information to steal your identity for personal or financial gain. With your personal information, scammers will try to: access your bank account and withdraw money set up new bank accounts in your name take out loans or sign up to credit cards in your name sign up to phone plans or other services purchase expensive goods in your name steal funds from your superannuation access your government online services such as MyGov access your email to find more information or impersonate you access your social media accounts to impersonate you – scamming your family and friends. How to avoid a scam Scammers target people of all backgrounds and ages. Always be alert to scams, especially online, and try keep personal information private. Scammers often use a sense of urgency to put pressure on people to send money and will often try to build trust with you over time. Simple ways to protect your personal information and avoid scams: If you are contacted by someone purporting to be from your bank, telecommunications company or a government agency, before giving access to any of your personal details, contact the relevant body to check it was them who contacted you. If someone is putting pressure on you to send funds or a deal seems too good to be true, you should pause; take time to review the suggestion and maybe discuss it with a trusted family member, friend or adviser. Never send money or give credit card details to someone you don’t know, or a business you do not trust. Always check a business is legitimate before sending funds or personal details to it – such as by searching its name on the ACCC’s Scamwatch website or ASIC’s MoneySmart website. Never send online account details or copies of personal documents to anyone you don’t know or trust. Lock your mailbox and use passwords for email. Shred sensitive documents and store any digital versions using security software. Do not forward or open strange or unreliable emails or website links. Check your credit report at least once a year. You can use a reputable credit reference bureau to help you monitor a

title: Search published decisions

Simply click the link and the PDF will open as normal. Copyright notice: Any person is authorised to view, copy, print and reproduce the Australian Financial Complaints Authority Limited (AFCA)’s published de-identified determinations for personal information and/or other non-commercial purposes. Notwithstanding the above, AFCA permits published de-identified determinations to be reproduced or published provided that: Public reporting of determinations any reproduction or publication of the determination is completely unaltered AFCA is acknowledged as maker and author of the determination, the document or web page reproducing the material includes an active link to AFCA’s website address (www.afca.org.au) and an active link to the AFCA web page hosting the full published determination any analysis or commentary about the determination is clearly attributable to the author of that analysis or commentary, and not AFCA, and is readily distinguishable from the content of the determination the reproduction or publication does not also contain material which is inconsistent with the de-identification of the determination.

title: Shail Singh speech at PritchittBland Communications event
body: AFCA Lead Ombudsman for Investments and Advice, Shail Singh, presented a speech to industry at the PritchittBland Communications New Year Kick-off event on 29 January 2026. Introduction Good evening, everyone, and thank you for the warm welcome. I want to begin by acknowledging the Traditional Custodians of the lands on which we meet today, the Gadigal people of the Eora Nation. I pay my respects to their Elders past and present, and extend that respect to all First Nations people joining us. It is an absolute pleasure to join you for PritchittBland’s New Year Kick-Off – one of the first major opportunities each year for our sector to gather and set the tone for the year ahead. For those of us at AFCA, 2025 was a year marked by enormous demand, significant regulatory change, and for many consumers, profound financial loss. And as we look ahead to 2026, the environment is only becoming more complex. Today, I want to give you a view of the complaints we’re seeing across the financial system, outline AFCA’s priorities for the year ahead, and then spend most of our time on one of the most significant failures the investments and advice sector has seen in years: the collapse of the Shield and First Guardian Master Funds. This collapse has devastated thousands of Australians. It has revealed deep structural issues. And it’s a reminder that when things go wrong in financial services, they go wrong in a way that affects real lives, real families, and real futures. Complaint trends in 2025 2025 was AFCA’s busiest calendar year on record. We received approximately 110,000 complaints and resolved around the same number of cases – a 14% increase on our previous record. We also saw $643 million in compensation flow back to consumers and small businesses. These numbers reflect both rising complexity and increasing expectations on the financial system. Key sector trends Looking at complaints by sector… That spike was driven primarily by the collapse of the Shield and First Guardian funds. Our complaint top issues tell their own story: For investments and advice, the story was even clearer: misleading information, poor advice, incorrect risk profiling, and failures in due diligence. Banking and finance remained our largest volume with 60,000 complaints, up 6%. General insurance jumped significantly – around 36,000 complaints, up 20%. Superannuation complaints increased 29% – a sharp rise after a period of relative stability. Life insurance complaints rose 13%. And in the area most relevant to this audience – Investments and Advice – we saw an extraordinary 58% increase, reaching more than 5,800 complaints. Delays in claims handling Misleading product or service information, up 110% Denial of claims Fee and charge disputes And service quality issues across the board. AFCA’s priorities in 2026 Turning now to our priorities this year, 2026 is significant for AFCA, not just in volume but in scope. We have major jurisdictional expansions coming: From March 2026 – com

title: Shield and First Guardian collapse – how AFCA can help
body: Lodge a complaint with AFCA If you are ready to make a complaint with AFCA, you can lodge a complaint online using the AFCA portal, here. If you are unable to lodge a complaint online or need additional help, you can submit a complaint via email (info@afca.org.au) or by calling us (1800 931 678). You can also visit the Super Consumers Australia website Take Your Super Back, a dedicated resource for consumers impacted by the Shield and First Guardian collapse. If your complaint relates to Interprac Financial Planning, please read an update from AFCA here. AFCA webinar: Shield and First Guardian collapse – how AFCA can help AFCA held a webinar on Thursday 23 October for investors impacted by the collapse of the Shield and First Guardian Master Funds. The webinar was recorded and can be watched below. We have also published answers to the questions raised during the webinar. These can be found here. To see the webinar presentation slides, click here. This page provides information for consumers impacted by the collapse of the Shield Master Fund and the First Guardian Master Fund, including those that have concerns about the financial advice they received or about their superannuation trustee. We encourage anyone who thinks they may have been impacted by the collapse of First Guardian and Shield Master Fund to seek advice. It is open to you to submit an AFCA complaint about the financial firm, or firms, and/or superannuation trustees that you have received products or services from, if you have concerns. All Australian Financial Services Licensees who provide services to retail clients are required to have internal dispute resolution systems in place. You can submit your complaint to AFCA without having first raised it with the financial firm. If you do this, AFCA will usually refer your complaint to the financial firm (or its external administrators) to see if the complaint can be resolved before investigating. For insolvent financial firms these are unlikely to resolve at internal dispute resolution. Understanding your options If you’ve been impacted by a financial firm that has gone into external administration and suffered a loss – you may still be able to submit a complaint to AFCA, but timing and accuracy are critical. It’s important to remember that AFCA is one avenue for potential redress. Other options available to you might include lodging a proof of debt with the liquidator/administrator, remediation programs, class actions or private actions in the courts. You can read our Factsheet about class actions affecting AFCA complaints here. What financial firms and/or superannuation trustees can I submit a complaint about? AFCA can only accept complaints about firms that are current members of our scheme. In these matters, financial firm members are often Australian Financial Services Licensees (AFSLs) (which may authorise advisers), the Responsible Entities (REs) of Managed Investment Schemes (MIS) and superannuation trustees. Individual financ
